meta.title: Benchmarks v2 (use-case explorer + honest model picker) design panel
meta.method: multi-agent design panel: 1 survey, 3 research lenses, 4 independent designer proposals, 4 adversarial cross-critiques, 1 synthesis, 2 adversarial verifiers (repo-grounding + trust-integrity), 1 self-repair pass
meta.note: Mirrors the shape of benchmarks-design-research.json (research, panel, synthesis). Zero em or en dashes.

research.lens: Decision-support and model-selection UX: how products help a non-expert pick a tool for a task WITHOUT manufacturing a single false winner, and how a "best for X" recommendation stays honest and useful when the underlying evidence is thin, dated, or self-reported.

patterns.name: Goal-to-Evidence Routing (decide WHICH benchmark before WHO leads)
patterns.description: The first job of a model-selection surface is not to announce a winner, it is to route the user from a fuzzy goal to the right yardstick. Wirecutter, Consumer Reports, and benchlm.ai all front a 'what are you trying to do' framing before any ranking: pick the task, then learn which 2 to 3 measures actually bear on it, then read who leads on each. The pick is the last beat, not the first. For us, the use-case page should open with task framing plus an 'evidence you are standing on' line ('Coding ability is read mainly through SWE-bench Verified, with LiveCodeBench and HumanEval as cross-checks') before any leader name appears, so the student leaves knowing the yardstick even when the leader is contested or empty.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Our locked rule forbids a composite winner, so the durable takeaway has to be benchmark literacy, not a name. Routing makes the benchmark choice the product. It also gracefully survives our data reality: a use case with 6 honest-empty boards still teaches the student which measures to trust, which is a complete answer even with zero live leaders.

patterns.name: Receipted Recommendation (a pick is a claim plus its evidence, never a verdict)
patterns.description: Every honest recommender pairs the suggestion with the single citation it rests on, inline, so the user can audit it. NerdWallet's 'best for' rows always read 'best for X because Y, as measured by Z'; Wirecutter footnotes the test that produced each pick. The recommendation degrades to its receipt: if you strip the bold name you are left with a true, dated, sourced sentence. Our honest decision pick is exactly this shape: label ('Best for factual recall') plus the one benchmark, the verbatim score string, the 'Mon YYYY' date, and the caveat, rendered with the same welded ScoreStamp receipt the detail rail already uses. The pick may claim only 'top of THIS board on THIS date'; it may never claim 'best model' in general.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: This is the literal text of James's locked decision ('Best for factual recall: model X, top of SimpleQA, May 2026, self-reported'). Reusing the ScoreStamp atom means the pick inherits the trust spine for free: the date, the baseline gloss, and the SELF-REPORTED or DISPUTED chip travel WITH the name, so a thin or self-reported pick literally cannot be displayed without its own disclaimer.

patterns.name: Confidence-Graded Picks (the strength of the claim tracks the strength of the evidence)
patterns.description: Honest recommenders do not present every pick at the same volume. Consumer Reports distinguishes a 'Recommended' from a 'CR Best Buy' from a 'no clear winner this year'; weather and forecasting UIs show confidence bands, not just a single number. The visual weight of a recommendation should scale with the trust tier of the board behind it: a live, independent board earns a confident pick; a near_ceiling, contested, or dated board earns a hedged one ('leans toward', 'as of the April board'); an honest-empty board earns no pick at all, replaced by a stated gap. Map this onto the existing four trust tiers rather than inventing a new scale.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: We have a STATUS tier (live | near_ceiling | contested | dated) on every benchmark already, plus 6 honest-empty and 2 self-reported cases. Grading pick confidence by that existing tier means a DATED board like BFCL (April 2026, predates the newest models) renders as a softer pick than EQ-Bench's live board, using only PR1 tokens (gold caveat hue, the DATED stone chip) and no new chrome. The trust tier becomes the throttle on how loud any pick is allowed to be.

patterns.name: Honest Negative Space (the absence of a pick is itself the finding)
patterns.description: The strongest trust signal a recommender can send is to refuse to recommend when the evidence is not there. Wirecutter publishes 'we don't recommend anything in this category yet' and explains why; good dashboards render an explicit empty state, never a blank or a zero. For thin categories the page should say, in full editorial voice, 'No board here measures this cleanly, so we are not naming a winner', then hand the student the single dated anchor and the reason (JS-only board, no independent eval, self-reported with a maintainer conflict). The honest-empty state is a first-class layout, parallel in weight to a populated pick row, not a faded fallback.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: PR1 already ships a first-class honest-empty state; this pattern extends it from the per-benchmark rail to the use-case pick block. With LongBench v2 and Global-MMLU honest-empty and OmniDocBench self-reported with a maintainer conflict, several use cases will have few or no live leaders. Treating that as designed content (the gap is the lesson) turns our biggest data weakness into the clearest demonstration of the skepticism mission.

patterns.name: Per-Goal Disambiguation (split one 'best' into several honest sub-bests)
patterns.description: benchlm's 'How to choose' block answers 'minimizing hallucinations? -> A; vertical accuracy on a budget? -> B; knowledge-heavy Q&A? -> C', refusing a single overall winner by decomposing the goal into sub-goals, each with its own honest pick. This is the legitimate alternative to a composite score: instead of averaging non-comparable boards into one fake number, you keep them separate and let the student's specific sub-goal select the board. The how-to-choose block is a short decision list, one line per sub-goal, each line pointing at exactly one benchmark and its caveat.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: It is the sanctioned escape hatch from the locked-out composite. Where benchmarks within a use case disagree (EQ-Bench's Elo-vs-Rubric split, where gpt-5.5 leads the Rubric but trails on Elo) we surface BOTH framings as different sub-goals rather than collapsing them into a false ranking, which is precisely the metric-disagreement honesty the page exists to teach. No averaging, no overall score, fully within the locked decisions.

patterns.name: Scaffold-vs-Model Disclosure (name what was actually measured)
patterns.description: A recommendation is dishonest if the thing tested is not the thing the user will buy. Reproducibility and MLPerf-style reporting always state the harness, the configuration, and whether the score reflects the bare component or a tuned system. Computer-use boards are the sharp case: OSWorld-Verified's leaders are agent scaffolds (Pointer Agent on Opus 4.7), not bare models, and a student calls a model, not the whole rig. The pick card must carry a one-line 'what was tested' disclosure when the tested unit differs from the model the student would pick, so the user is never misled into thinking a system score is a model score.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: OSWorld is explicitly flagged in our research as scaffolds-not-models, and BFCL distinguishes function-calling modes (FC vs Prompt). Without this disclosure a student reads '83.6%' and attributes it to a model they cannot actually invoke. A small caveat line in the existing gold hue keeps the pick honest without a price/speed/latency catalog (which is locked out), and it reinforces that the number describes a setup, not a verdict.

patterns.name: Near-Tie Suppression (refuse a rank order the evidence cannot support)
patterns.description: When the top entries are within noise, an honest recommender stops ordering them and says so. Election forecasters report 'too close to call'; sports and Elo systems publish confidence intervals that overlap. Presenting #1 over #2 when they are a fraction of a point apart manufactures a winner the data does not justify. The pick should show a near-tie note ('claude-fable-5 and claude-opus-4-7 are within 12.5 Elo, effectively tied at the top') instead of a crisp single name when the margin is inside the board's resolution.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: PR1 already has a near-tie note primitive, and our verified data is riddled with near-ties: EQ-Bench #1/#2 are 12.5 Elo apart, BFCL #2/#3 are 0.73 points apart, OSWorld's top three sit within 2 points. Carrying the near-tie note into the pick block stops the honest picker from re-introducing the false-winner problem the whole page is built to avoid, and it is a direct reuse of an existing primitive with no new chrome.

references.name: Wirecutter (New York Times) buying-guide model
references.why: Borrow the 'best for X' decomposition, the always-cited 'why we picked it' receipt, and especially the willingness to publish 'we don't recommend anything here yet'. This is the gold standard for honest negative space and receipted recommendation. Adapt the voice to editorial trust-first; ignore its affiliate-commerce framing.

references.name: Consumer Reports ratings and recommendation tiers
references.why: Borrow graded recommendation labels (Recommended vs Best Buy vs none) and transparent test methodology as the trust spine. This maps cleanly onto confidence-graded picks driven by our four trust tiers. Avoid its single overall score per product, which is exactly the composite we have locked out.

references.name: benchlm.ai use-case explorer and per-use-case pages
references.why: Borrow the information design: the explore-by-use-case grid, the dated bottom-line sentence, the 'How to choose' per-goal block, and decision-ready picks. ADAPT to trust-first by attaching a receipt and caveat to every pick. REJECT its single overall score and any crown-the-winner framing; that is our negative reference.

references.name: Artificial Analysis model comparison
references.why: Borrow the clarity of per-task framing and the discipline of dating every figure. REJECT the unified Model x Benchmark mega-table with a composite Score column, the price/speed/context catalog, and the AI-race leaderboard crown; these are precisely the non-comparable averaging and model-catalog moves locked out of this surface.

references.name: FiveThirtyEight forecasting and 'too close to call' presentation
references.why: Borrow the discipline of expressing uncertainty as a first-class output: confidence bands, explicit ties, and never ordering what the data cannot order. Directly informs near-tie suppression and confidence-graded picks. Their numbers-with-humility voice is the right register for our caveat-forward picks.

references.name: MLPerf / MLCommons benchmark reporting rules
references.why: Borrow the requirement to disclose the exact configuration and harness behind any number, and the open/closed-division split that separates tuned systems from bare components. Directly informs scaffold-vs-model disclosure for OSWorld and BFCL. Avoid its engineering density; we want one plain caveat line, not a results manifest.

references.name: Nielsen Norman Group, comparison tables and decision-support UX guidance
references.why: Borrow the usability findings on how non-experts scan comparisons, why parallel structure lowers cognitive load, and why a clear 'recommended for you' beats raw data dumps for novices. Grounds the audience-fit reasoning. Avoid the implication that more rows and columns is better; our audience needs fewer, framed choices.

pitfalls: Re-manufacturing a single winner through the back door: ranking picks across use cases, adding a 'most-picked model' tally, or letting a model that tops several boards read as the overall champion. Any cross-use-case aggregation reintroduces the composite the page exists to reject.

Receipt drift: showing a pick's name in bold while the date, source, and caveat sit in faint secondary text or behind a hover. For a non-technical student the bold name is all they read; if the caveat is not welded to the name (ScoreStamp-style), the honesty is decorative, not functional.

Treating honest-empty as an error state: rendering a thin use case as a faded, apologetic, or visually smaller block. Six honest-empty boards must look like deliberate, full-weight editorial content, or students read absence as a bug and discount the whole surface.

Self-reported laundering: displaying OmniDocBench's self-reported top-3 (with a maintainer who shares an org with the #1 model) at the same visual confidence as an independent board. Without a loud SELF-REPORTED chip plus the conflict caveat on the pick itself, the page becomes the aggregator it was built to flag.

Scaffold-as-model confusion: presenting OSWorld's 83.6% as a model score when it is an agent scaffold the student cannot invoke, or hiding BFCL's FC-vs-Prompt mode. The student walks away believing a number about a thing they cannot actually use.

Staleness passed off as current: rendering BFCL's April 2026 board (predating Opus 4.7/4.8, Gemini 3.1, GPT-5.5) with a crisp present-tense 'the best tool-use model is X'. A dated board needs an as-of frame and the DATED tier styling, or it silently lies about the present.

False precision on near-ties: ordering EQ-Bench #1 over #2 (12.5 Elo apart) or BFCL #2 over #3 (0.73 points) as a clean ranking. Crowning inside the noise band is the exact false-winner failure the trust spine forbids, just relocated into the pick block.

Choice overload and jargon creep: dumping all benchmarks for a use case as equals, or carrying raw metric names (Overall Acc, Elo, Rubric, AST) without a plain gloss. Mixed non-technical students need 2 to 3 framed yardsticks with everyday language, not an undifferentiated wall of acronyms.
research.audienceFit: The audience is university students from mixed, often non-technical backgrounds, each arriving with a concrete task and one question: which model do I pick. That framing is a gift and a trap. The gift: a goal-first surface (route the student from task to the right 2 to 3 yardsticks, then to a caveated leader) matches exactly how a novice shops, and it lets the durable takeaway be benchmark literacy rather than a model name that will be stale in a month. The trap: a non-expert reads the bold winner and skips everything else, so any honesty that is not physically welded to the recommendation (date, source, self-reported chip, scaffold disclosure, near-tie note, all traveling WITH the name via the ScoreStamp atom) is honesty they will never see. For this audience the design must do two things at once: make picking easy by reducing each task to a few framed choices in plain language, and make over-trust hard by grading every pick's confidence to its board's trust tier and rendering thin, dated, self-reported, and honest-empty cases as full-weight, clearly-labeled editorial content rather than gaps or footnotes. Done right, a student leaves not just with a defensible pick for today but with the more valuable skill the TCO club is actually teaching: how to tell whether a number about an AI model can be trusted at all."

research.lens: Faceted / use-case browse and explorer surfaces: card grids versus editorial hairline indexes for a bounded, knowable set of tasks, mapping a student task taxonomy to a scannable entry point without ever implying cross-benchmark comparability or a league table. References benchlm.ai and Artificial Analysis as shapes to adapt or reject.

patterns.name: Bounded-set hairline index over an infinite-scroll card wall
patterns.description: Eleven use cases is a small, knowable, authored set, so render the explorer as a single-column editorial hairline list (the PR1 BenchmarkRow grammar: a tile-indigo lead glyph, name as hero at --text-xl, a one-line audience gloss in --color-text-2, an uppercase tracked meta strip) rather than the 4-column benchlm card wall. One column forces vertical reading, reuses the PR1 .editorial-link sweep and bench-row entrance, and inherits the <=600px collapse for free. Cards across a row invite a visual A/B scan; a hairline column reads like a contents page, not a scoreboard.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The PR1 panel already rejected a two-up grid for the benchmark index precisely because a row of cells reads as a league table. The use-case explorer carries the same risk one level up: a grid of eleven equal cards silently implies the use cases are ranked, comparable peers. A hairline list says these are eleven doors, not eleven contestants, and it costs zero new chrome because BenchmarkRow is already the template.

patterns.name: Backing-benchmarks-as-receipts on every facet entry
patterns.description: benchlm lists source benchmarks beneath each use-case card. Adopt that, but make the list a trust artifact, not decoration: under each use-case name print the 2 to 3 ordered benchmark names, each wearing its PR1 StatusChip (live / near ceiling / contested / dated) at size sm. The reader sees, before clicking, that Long documents rests on one DATED anchor while Coding has three live boards. The facet entry previews its own evidentiary weight.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Our locked data reality is that 6 of 22 benchmarks are honest-empty and 2 more are self-reported-flagged, so many facets are thin. Surfacing the backing chips at the explorer level turns thinness into an honest, scannable signal instead of a disappointment discovered only after a click. It reuses StatusChip verbatim and keeps STATUS the hero one altitude higher than PR1.

patterns.name: Task framing as the facet label, model names withheld until cited
patterns.description: Each facet is named for the STUDENT TASK in plain language (Factual accuracy: not making things up; Writing and communication), with a one-line audienceLine for a non-technical reader. No model name, no score, and no benchmark jargon appears on the explorer surface; the first time a model is named is inside a use-case page honest pick, where it arrives welded to its date and caveat. The explorer answers what is my task and defers which model to the receipted layer.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Honest-picks-only means a model name is never allowed to float free of its benchmark, date, and caveat. Keeping all model names off the explorer enforces that at the architecture level: there is no surface where a bare best-model string can leak. It also fits the mixed, non-technical audience, who recognize their task long before they recognize a model id.

patterns.name: Honest-empty facets are first-class, not filtered out
patterns.description: Artificial Analysis and benchlm only show categories that have a clean leader; a thin or empty category quietly disappears. Invert this: a facet with no trustworthy live board (Long documents and big context, Languages and translation, optionally Studying and tutoring) stays in the index at full weight, its backing chip reading DATED or its row carrying a No trustworthy current board gloss. The PR1 honest-empty state becomes the body of that use-case page.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The mission says the honesty must be a FEATURE, not a hole. A taxonomy that hides its empty facets teaches the opposite lesson, that every task has a confident answer. Showing the gap, with the PR1 honest-empty treatment as the destination, makes the absence of a leader a teachable moment and keeps the eleven-facet promise intact.

patterns.name: Decision-ready picks as receipt cards, not a ranked podium
patterns.description: Borrow benchlm decision-ready pick cards, but reshape each into the welded ScoreStamp grammar: line 1 the pick label (Best for minimizing hallucinations), line 2 model + lab, line 3 the verbatim score string + baseline gloss, line 4 the normalized Mon YYYY source with inline SELF-REPORTED / DISPUTED chips. Picks are laid out as a hairline-separated column, never a 1-2-3 podium with medals or deltas, and never more than the benchmarks of that facet can honestly support (often one, sometimes zero).
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: This is the literal locked card shape: every best-for names one benchmark, one date, one caveat. ScoreStamp already enforces the three-lines-never-separate rule, so reusing it makes a pick physically unable to claim a number without its receipt. Avoiding the podium and the vs N delta kills the league-table read that benchlm Full Rankings list invites.

patterns.name: How-to-choose as a per-goal branch, not a winner declaration
patterns.description: benchlm How to choose block maps a single use case to several sub-goals (Minimizing hallucinations -> A; knowledge-heavy Q&A -> B). Keep the branching structure but strip every axis we are forbidden to claim: no budget, speed, latency, or context-window branches (those need the rejected model catalog). Branches split only on TASK shape that a benchmark actually measures, and each branch lands on a receipted pick or an honest no clean answer yet.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The how-to-choose block is where a student converts a task into a benchmark to watch, which is exactly our literacy goal. Pruning the cost and speed branches keeps it inside honest-picks-only and the no-model-catalog rule, while preserving the useful move: teaching that short factual recall and knowledge Q&A are different tests with different leaders.

patterns.name: Cross-tagging shown as shared membership, not double-counting
patterns.description: A benchmark can sit in multiple facets (Humanity Last Exam in Science and Factuality; LMArena in Preference and Writing). Render this as honest shared membership: the same benchmark appears under each relevant facet with the same chip and same dated leader, and the use-case page can note also central to Science as a quiet editorial cross-link via the PR1 sweep. Never aggregate a benchmark appearances into a how many leaderboards this model tops tally.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Cross-tagging is the moment a naive design would invent a composite (tops 4 of 11 use cases), which is exactly the self-reported-aggregate move this page exists to flag. Treating multi-tag as plain shared membership keeps each appearance independently receipted and uses the existing relatedConcepts cross-link vocabulary rather than new chrome.

references.name: benchlm.ai (Explore by Use Case grid + per-use-case page)
references.why: Borrow the information architecture: a use-case entry point, backing benchmarks under each facet, a dated bottom-line sentence, decision-ready picks, a what changed section, and a per-goal how-to-choose block. Reject its 4-column card wall in favor of our hairline index, reject the composite overall score, and reject the Full Rankings list with score bars and vs N deltas, which are the league-table read we are locked out of.

references.name: Artificial Analysis (models index)
references.why: Useful as a negative reference. Borrow nothing structural: its single composite Intelligence Index, its cost/speed/context catalog columns, and its leaderboard crown are precisely the locked-out moves (no composite, no model catalog, no AI-race framing). Studying it clarifies what our explorer must NOT become as it grows.

references.name: Nielsen Norman Group: Faceted Search and Filters
references.why: Grounds the choice of facets-as-navigation for a bounded, authored set. Borrow the guidance that facets should be mutually intelligible, non-overlapping in intent, and that a small known taxonomy is better surfaced as labeled entry points than as an open filter UI. Confirms our eleven authored use cases belong as a contents-page index, not a dynamic filter panel.

references.name: GOV.UK Design System: task-based navigation and start pages
references.why: Model for task-framed entry points written for a mixed, non-expert audience. Borrow the plain-language task labeling, the one-line description under each entry, and the discipline of one clear next action per page. Supports framing facets by the student task (not making things up) rather than by benchmark name.

references.name: Stripe Docs landing (use-case cards over a hairline grid)
references.why: Reference for editorial restraint in a use-case grid: typographic hierarchy, generous hairline separation, hover affordances without shadows or heavy cards. Borrow the calm, no-chrome treatment that lets the label and its one-line gloss carry the row, which matches our editorial-surface voice and the no-new-card-system constraint.

references.name: NYT Wirecutter best-for guidance structure
references.why: Borrow the honest best for a specific need framing: a named pick tied to a stated reason and a tested basis, plus explicit we did not find a clear winner admissions. Reject the star-rating composite. Validates honest-picks-only: a pick is trustworthy when it names its single basis and date, and weak when it hides behind an aggregate.

pitfalls: Implying the eleven facets are ranked or comparable: a uniform grid of equal cards reads as a leaderboard of tasks. Order them by an editorial logic (most-backed first, honest-empty grouped last) and never number them as ranks.

Letting a model name appear on the explorer surface without its receipt: any top model X teaser on a facet card violates honest-picks-only, because the date and caveat cannot fit there. Withhold all model names until the receipted ScoreStamp layer.

Hiding thin or empty facets to make the grid look full: dropping Long documents, Languages, or Studying because they lack a clean leader teaches that every task has a confident answer, the opposite of the mission.

Reintroducing a composite through the back door: a tops N of 11 use cases tally, a per-facet aggregate, or a most-awarded model summary is the self-reported aggregate this page exists to flag, even if no single number is shown.

Smuggling the model catalog back via how-to-choose branches: on a budget, fastest, or biggest context branches require price/speed/context data we rejected. Branch only on task shape a benchmark actually measures.

Letting OSWorld-style agent-scaffold leaders read as bare-model picks: a non-technical student will read Pointer Agent w/ Opus 4.7 as a model. The pick card must make the scaffold-not-model caveat unmissable, or it teaches a false equivalence.

Over-faceting or duplicating into apparent abundance: cross-tagged benchmarks (HLE, LMArena) appearing in several facets can look like more evidence than exists. Show shared membership honestly and never let one board reuse inflate a sense of coverage.

Building a dynamic filter UI for a static, authored set: a knowable eleven-item taxonomy does not need a faceted-search filter panel; that chrome implies an open, queryable dataset and clashes with the editorial hairline voice and the no-new-chrome rule.

Treating a DATED or April-2026 board (BFCL) as current: a freshness caveat quieter than the score lets a stale leader masquerade as today answer. The date and tier must sit inside the welded stamp, never below the fold.
research.audienceFit: The audience is university students from mixed, often non-technical backgrounds, each arriving with a task and no vocabulary for benchmarks or model ids. That makes the use-case label, not the benchmark name, the only reliable entry point, so the explorer must read like a plain-language contents page (not making things up; writing and communication) where a student recognizes their own task in one scan. A card-wall scoreboard would invite exactly the wrong instinct for this group: pick the highest number. The hairline index plus receipted picks instead teaches the literacy move we want, which is to ask which test, how fresh, and can I trust it before trusting any model. Crucially, the honest-empty facets serve this audience best of all: a non-expert who learns that studying and tutoring or long context has no trustworthy board has learned more about how to read AI claims than one handed a confident but hollow ranking. The surface succeeds when a student leaves knowing their task, the one benchmark to watch, its date, and the single caveat that qualifies it, and never leaves believing in an overall best model.

research.lens: Designing for absence and uncertainty: making "no trustworthy leader yet", "dated anchor only", and "self-reported only" first-class teaching moments rather than holes, by borrowing sparse-data honesty patterns from data journalism, dashboards, and forecasting and applying them to a use-case picker where most use cases will surface few or no live leaders.

patterns.name: Absence As Content, Not Null State
patterns.description: Treat every empty or untrustworthy cell as a sentence the page wants to say, not a layout failure to apologize for. Each honest-empty use case gets the same slot geometry as a populated one (eyebrow, lead, anchor line) but the leader rail is replaced by an authored explanation of WHY no number survived: the board is JS-rendered and unreadable, no independent leaderboard exists, or every claim was self-reported. Pair this with a single dated baseline anchor (human 53.7 percent, chance 25 percent) so the row still teaches calibration even with zero leaders. The PR1 honest-empty state already proves this on the detail page; the use-case surface inherits it verbatim rather than inventing a grey placeholder.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Our data reality is that 6 of 22 benchmarks are honest-empty and 2 more are self-reported-flagged, so LongBench v2, Global-MMLU, SimpleQA, MMMU-Pro, and Design2Code use cases will open with no live leader. Locked decisions forbid inventing a composite to paper over the gap, so absence has to carry its own weight. Making it authored content turns the most common state on the page into the clearest demonstration of the trust-first spine.

patterns.name: The Receipt-Bearing Pick (Provenance Welded To The Claim)
patterns.description: Every honest decision pick is a welded unit where the claim and its receipts cannot be separated, mirroring the PR1 ScoreStamp atom: line 1 the claim (Best for tool use: Claude Opus 4.5), line 2 the single benchmark plus verbatim score plus normalized Mon YYYY date (top of BFCL V4, 77.47 percent Overall Acc, Apr 2026), line 3 the caveat that the pick rests on (board predates the newest models, treat as dated). A pick may never claim more than its one cited row supports, and the caveat is structural, not optional copy. No pick without a date, no pick without a named benchmark, no pick that averages across rows.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: This is the literal shape of the honest-picks-only locked decision (name the single benchmark, the date, the caveat). Welding the caveat to the claim makes it impossible to screenshot the flattering half (the model name) without the qualifying half (dated, self-reported), which is exactly the self-reported-aggregate move this page exists to flag. It reuses the ScoreStamp three-line grammar with zero new chrome.

patterns.name: Trust-Tier Gating Of The Pick Verb
patterns.description: The strength of the language in a pick is bound to the benchmark's STATUS tier, not chosen freelance. live -> can say leads or tops; near_ceiling -> say leads but with a saturation note; contested or self-reported -> downgrade to claims to lead or self-reports the top spot plus a conflict caveat; dated -> led as of Mon YYYY; honest-empty -> no pick is emitted at all, the slot instead reads no trustworthy leader yet and explains why. A near-tie at the top demotes a single-winner pick to a co-leaders framing. The verb is a deterministic function of trust state, authored once in a small lookup the way TRUST_META maps status to chip color.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: OmniDocBench is self-reported AND its number-1 shares a maintainer with the benchmark, BFCL is dated April 2026, EQ-Bench has a real near-tie at 1 and 2 plus an Elo-versus-Rubric disagreement. A single confident verb across all of these would lie. Gating the verb to the tier means the same component renders honestly across the whole messy roster and keeps STATUS, not the score, as the hero of the pick just as it is the hero of every PR1 row.

patterns.name: Anchor-Only Fallback (Calibration Without A Winner)
patterns.description: Borrowed from data journalism and forecasting dashboards: when you cannot show a current value, show the reference frame instead. Where no live leader exists, the use case still renders a single most-cited dated anchor as a quiet stone-tier line (human experts 53.7 percent, best direct model 50.1 percent at last reading, chance 25 percent). The reader leaves knowing what good looks like and how hard the task is, even though nobody can be crowned. This is strictly a baseline anchor, never dressed up as a ranking.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The research already supplies these anchors for the honest-empty cases (LongBench v2 human 53.7 / direct 50.1 / chance 25; Global-MMLU 42 languages, 28 percent culturally sensitive). It lets a use case page teach calibration and difficulty even when the leader rail is empty, converting a dead end into the WHAT TO WATCH lesson the PR1 design already values, with no invented number.

patterns.name: Honesty Density Made Visible Up Front
patterns.description: On the use-case explorer grid, each card surfaces a small, scannable signal of how well-evidenced that use case is BEFORE the student commits a click: a count of live-backed benchmarks versus total (2 of 3 live), or a tier glyph row reusing the existing StatusChip palette. A use case backed only by honest-empty or self-reported boards is allowed to look thin, and that thinness is the warning. A top-right Browse all benchmarks link mirrors benchlm without importing its confident-everywhere tone.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: It manages expectations honestly: a student picking the Long context or Multilingual card learns from the grid itself that the evidence is sparse, so the empty page is not a bait-and-switch. It turns the uneven data reality (many use cases with few live leaders) into a literacy signal the moment the page loads, and it reuses the StatusChip tokens rather than adding a new badge system.

patterns.name: Scaffold-Versus-Model Disclosure Lane
patterns.description: When a leaderboard entry is not a bare model the student can call (an agent scaffold, a harness, a system), the pick carries an explicit what you are actually looking at line that separates the system from the underlying model. OSWorld-Verified number-1 is Pointer Agent with Opus 4.7, not Opus 4.7 alone; the pick says led by a scaffold (Pointer Agent) running Opus 4.7, you cannot get this score from the bare model. This is a named disclosure lane, reusing the gold caveat hue and the inline-chip pattern (like SELF-REPORTED and DISPUTED).
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Our audience is non-technical students who will read a model name as something they can sign up for and use. The Computer use use case is built on OSWorld scaffolds, so without this lane the pick is actively misleading. It extends the existing inline-chip vocabulary (a SCAFFOLD chip alongside SELF-REPORTED and DISPUTED) instead of inventing chrome, and it is the single most important caveat type the new roster introduces.

patterns.name: Metric-Disagreement As A First-Class Note
patterns.description: When two legitimate metrics rank the same field differently, show the disagreement rather than silently picking the flattering one. The pick states which metric it ranks by and flags the dissent: ranked by Elo (head-to-head preference); note gpt-5.5 leads the Rubric score yet trails on Elo, so the order depends on which you trust. Rendered as a near-tie-style note, reusing the PR1 near-tie line treatment.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: EQ-Bench Creative Writing is our ONLY verified independent live top-3 for writing, and it carries exactly this Elo-versus-Rubric split with a near-tie at 1 and 2. Hiding it would make our single best-evidenced use case quietly dishonest. Surfacing it teaches the core literacy lesson (a benchmark is a choice of metric, not a verdict) and reuses the existing near-tie note primitive.

references.name: FiveThirtyEight forecasts (uncertainty intervals and probabilistic framing)
references.why: Borrow the discipline of never showing a point estimate without its uncertainty: they lead with ranges, probabilities, and explicit can-change framing rather than a single confident number. Adapt their dated, qualified language to the pick caveats (led as of Mon YYYY; this may flip). Reject any move toward a single hero percentage; our locked decision bans the composite they would call an aggregate.

references.name: Our World in Data (missing-data handling and source-on-every-figure)
references.why: Best in class at making gaps legible: greyed or explicitly labeled no-data regions on maps, a visible source and date under every chart, and prose that explains WHY a series is missing. Borrow the source-and-date-welded-to-every-number habit (it maps onto ScoreStamp) and the explicit no-data-here labeling for our honest-empty use cases. This is the template for absence-as-content.

references.name: benchlm.ai use-case explorer and per-use-case pages
references.why: Borrow the information design: the Explore by use case grid, the dated bottom-line sentence, the decision-ready picks row, and the How to choose per-goal block. ADAPT to trust-first by gating every claim on STATUS and a caveat. REJECT their confident-everywhere tone and any implication that every use case has a clean winner; our grid must let sparse use cases look sparse.

references.name: Artificial Analysis (models index and rankings)
references.why: Useful reference for how a full rankings list reads (rank, model, provider badges, score bar, vs number-N delta). Borrow only the per-benchmark rankings list shape if we ever expand a single board. REJECT outright their unified composite Intelligence score, the cross-benchmark mega-table, and the price/speed/context catalog columns; those are precisely the locked-out, non-comparable averaging this page exists to flag.

references.name: Nielsen Norman Group, empty states guidance
references.why: Borrow the principle that an empty state should explain the situation and what it means, not just sit blank, and should never feel like an error when it is an expected condition. For us, honest-empty is the EXPECTED, most common state, so it must read as an informative, authored teaching moment with an anchor and a why, not as a broken page or a coming soon stub.

references.name: GOV.UK Design System, error and empty patterns plus plain-language voice
references.why: Borrow the plain-language, no-jargon honesty and the convention of telling users plainly what is and is not known. For a mixed non-technical student audience, the use-case pages need GOV.UK-grade clarity on caveats (dated, self-reported, scaffold) written as short declarative sentences, not hedged marketing copy. Reject any ornamental or reassuring tone that would soften a real caveat.

pitfalls: Coming-soon theatre: rendering honest-empty use cases as a greyed placeholder or a coming soon stub implies the data exists and is merely pending. For us the absence is permanent and meaningful (no independent board exists for Global-MMLU); a stub would erase the lesson and read as a broken feature to a non-technical student.

Silent winner laundering: emitting a confident Best for X pick from a contested or self-reported board (OmniDocBench, whose number-1 shares a maintainer with the benchmark) without the conflict caveat. The screenshot-able half becomes a false endorsement, which is the exact self-reported-aggregate failure this page is built to flag.

Stale data presented as current: showing the BFCL April 2026 top-3 with present-tense leads when the board predates Opus 4.7/4.8, Gemini 3.1, and GPT-5.5. Without a dated, past-tense frame the pick silently lies by omission to a student who assumes the site is live.

Scaffold-as-model confusion: letting OSWorld surface Pointer Agent with Opus 4.7 as if a student could pick and use Opus 4.7 to get 83.6 percent. Non-technical readers cannot infer that the score belongs to a whole system; without the scaffold disclosure lane the pick is actively misleading.

Caveat fatigue and uniform greyness: if every empty and dated and self-reported state looks identical and equally muted, students stop reading them and the distinctions (dated versus self-reported versus scaffold versus truly empty) collapse into one dismissible grey. The four states must remain visually and verbally distinct using the existing tier palette.

Cherry-picked metric to dodge a tie: quietly ranking EQ-Bench by whichever of Elo or Rubric produces a cleaner single winner, hiding that gpt-5.5 leads Rubric while trailing on Elo, and ignoring the 1/2 near-tie. This manufactures false certainty on our single best-evidenced use case and teaches the opposite of the literacy goal.

Honesty-density signal that punishes sparse use cases into invisibility: if the grid signal makes thin use cases look like errors or hides them, students never reach the most instructive pages (the ones that teach why a task is unmeasured). Sparse must look intentional and worth visiting, not broken.

Reintroducing a composite by the back door: summing or averaging the per-use-case picks into an implied overall standing, or letting the explorer grid imply one model wins everything. Any cross-use-case aggregate violates the locked honest-picks-only decision and recreates the rejected mega-table.
research.audienceFit: University students from mixed, often non-technical backgrounds arrive with a task and a hope for a single right answer, and our data reality cannot give them one: most use cases will show few or no trustworthy live leaders. The absence-and-uncertainty lens turns that mismatch into the lesson. Instead of a confident catalog that quietly launders stale, self-reported, or scaffold numbers into endorsements, these students get a page that says plainly, in short declarative sentences, what is known, how old it is, who measured it, and why some answers do not exist yet. Receipts welded to every claim (benchmark, date, caveat) and trust-tier-gated verbs mean a student cannot screenshot a flattering model name without its qualifier, which protects exactly the reader least equipped to add the caveat themselves. Anchor-only fallbacks let even an empty use case teach what good looks like and how hard a task is, and the honesty-density signal on the explorer grid sets honest expectations before the click. The net effect is literacy over lookup: a non-technical student leaves not just with a pick but with the durable habit of asking can I trust this number, which is the whole point of the TCO surface and survives long after any single leaderboard goes stale.

proposal.designLanguageName: The Contents Page (a hairline index, not a scoreboard)
proposal.oneLineThesis: A use case is a door, not a contestant: the explorer is a single-column hairline index in the exact BenchmarkRow idiom, and the use-case page is prose-led with honest picks rendered as inline ScoreStamp lines, so a model name can never appear without its benchmark, date, and caveat welded to it, and an absent leader reads as authored content, not a hole.
proposal.explorerSurface:
A single-column hairline index at --maxw-content (1040px), data-surface="editorial" inside the (main) data-theme="light" shell, built by copying the BenchmarkRow grammar verbatim into a new UseCaseRow. NOT a benchlm 4-column card wall: a row of equal cards reads as a league table of tasks (the exact read the PR1 panel rejected one level down), so eleven equal cards would silently imply the use cases are ranked, comparable peers. One column reads like a contents page.

Header (reusing the exact BenchmarksClient header block): SectionEyebrow "Pick a model for a task", h1 "By use case" at clamp(34px,4vw,44px) 600 -0.02em, then a 680px lead p in --color-text-2: "Start from what you are trying to do. Each task names the two or three tests that actually measure it, how far you can trust each, and who currently leads, dated and caveated. Where no test measures a task cleanly, that gap is the answer." A top-right editorial-link "All benchmarks ->" (the .editorial-link sweep + ArrowRight) routes to the PR1 Standings, mirroring benchlm's "Browse all rankings" without importing its confident tone.

Each UseCaseRow (grid 36px / minmax(0,1fr) / 172px, columnGap 20, full-row absolute Link to /benchmarks/use/[slug], identical to BenchmarkRow so it inherits the <=600px collapse and benchCellIn entrance for free):
- col 1: the tile-indigo ordinal badge (position only, 01..11, never rank quality), reused verbatim.
- col 2: use-case name as hero (.editorial-link .bench-name at --text-xl 600), then a one-line audienceLine in 14px --color-text-2 (truncated, plain language: "Will it make things up when you ask it a fact?"), then an 11px 0.08em uppercase --color-text-3 meta strip that lists the backing benchmark NAMES it draws on (e.g. "HUMANITY'S LAST EXAM . SIMPLEQA"). No model name, no score, ever, on this surface.
- col 3 (the right signal, replacing PR1's StatusChip+caveat): an "evidence" readout. A compact row of size="sm" StatusChip pills, one per backing benchmark, in TRUST_TAB_ORDER, so the reader sees BEFORE clicking that Coding shows three live-ish chips while Long context shows one DATED chip. Beneath, an 11px --color-text-2 honesty gloss computed from the backing set: "3 boards, 1 live" or, for thin cases, "No trustworthy current board". This makes thinness a scannable signal, not a post-click disappointment.

Ordering is editorial, never ranked: most-live-backed use cases first, honest-empty use cases grouped last under a quiet HairRule + an 11px --color-text-3 label "Tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet" so the empties read as a deliberate section, not failures. Optional "Studying and tutoring" lives here if James wants it.

A trust-filter tab row is NOT added here (the PR1 CategoryTab filters by trust tier, which is meaningless on a use-case axis). If a filter is ever wanted it would be a use-case grouping tab, deferred.
proposal.explorerLivesWhere:
REWORK, not bolt-on, but minimal. /benchmarks becomes a two-mode surface joined by one ghost-text toggle pair at the top of the existing header, reusing the CategoryTab visual (lifted from module-local to exported): "By use case" | "All benchmarks (The Standings)". Default landing = "By use case", because the locked audience arrives with a task, not a benchmark name; "All benchmarks" is the PR1 trust-first list, completely unchanged in its internals (its trust spine, BenchmarkRow, honest-empty state, and trust-filter tabs stay exactly as built). 

Implementation keeps PR1's surfaces intact: the toggle is a client tab in a thin new BenchmarksHub wrapper that renders either UseCaseExplorerClient or the unchanged BenchmarksClient. The PR1 page.tsx now also fetches use cases and passes both. Nothing about the PR1 row, sort (TRUST_ORDER), or visibility rule changes; the only edit to BenchmarksClient is exporting CategoryTab. The sidebar item stays the single /benchmarks (prefix-match keeps it active on /benchmarks/use/*). This honors "do not redesign the trust spine" while answering the brief's openness to reworking the surface: the trust list is reframed as one of two doors, not replaced.
proposal.useCasePageSurface:
/benchmarks/use/[slug]: a prose-led page reusing the .trend-detail-split two-column grid (minmax(0,1.5fr) 1px minmax(0,1fr), columnGap 48, collapses + hides rule at <=720px), .trend-detail-enter fade, and every left-column prose primitive copied from benchmark-detail-client (Beat, Prose, MicroLabel, ExampleTask, HairRule, SectionEyebrow). data-surface="editorial", --maxw-content.

LEFT COLUMN (the brief):
- BackLink variant pointing to /benchmarks (arrow-left + "By use case").
- Domain-style kicker: 11px 0.18em uppercase --color-text-3 "Use case", + DraftChip if the use-case content row is a draft.
- A bold task-framing lead at 19px 500 --color-text (the same lead treatment as the benchmark detail bus-stop sentence): "You want a model that will not make up facts." Then audienceLine prose beneath in Prose.
- Beat "Which tests measure this": Prose naming the 2-3 benchmarks in ordered prose with each name as an .editorial-link to /benchmarks/[slug], plus one clause each on how far to trust it ("SimpleQA is the canonical short-fact board, but no current top-3 survived verification, so treat it as dated; Humanity's Last Exam cross-checks expert knowledge but is contested"). This is the literacy payoff: the student leaves knowing the yardstick even when the leader is empty.
- Beat "How to choose": a per-goal branch list rendered eyebrow-over-prose (SectionEyebrow + a stack of MicroLabel-over-Prose pairs), one sub-goal per line, each landing on exactly one benchmark or an honest "no clean answer yet". Branches split ONLY on task shape a benchmark measures (e.g. "Short factual recall -> SimpleQA"; "Knowledge-heavy expert Q&A -> Humanity's Last Exam"). NO budget/speed/context branches (those need the locked-out catalog).
- WatchOut beat (the one gold recolor, reused verbatim) when the use case carries a cross-cutting caveat (e.g. for Agents: "Many of these boards score agent scaffolds, not bare models").
- GoDeeper cross-links to /concepts (validated the same way PR1 validates relatedConcepts).

RIGHT RAIL (the picks panel): a UseCasePickPanel mirroring LeaderPanel's shape: an 11px uppercase stamp "Honest picks" on a 1px --color-border hairline, then a hairline-separated COLUMN of pick lines (never a 1-2-3 podium, no medals, no vs-#N delta). Each pick is a PickStamp (see honestPickDesign). When the use case is honest-empty across all backing boards, the rail renders the PR1 HonestEmpty body verbatim ("No board here measures this cleanly, so we are not naming a winner") plus the single dated anchor, given the same slot weight as a populated rail. A PanelFooter-style line lists the backing benchmarks' StatusChips and a "These leaders move; dates are when each board was last readable" note.
proposal.howToChooseDesign:
Eyebrow-over-prose, not cards. SectionEyebrow "How to choose", then a vertical stack (gap 18) of branches. Each branch is a MicroLabel (the sub-goal, 11px uppercase --color-text-3, e.g. "MINIMIZING MADE-UP FACTS") directly over a Prose line that names one benchmark as an .editorial-link and states the honest landing ("lean on SimpleQA, but its current board failed verification, so this is a dated read, not a live leader"). Branches are authored in the use-case content layer as howToChoose: { goal, benchmarkSlug, guidance }[]. 

Hard rules the shape enforces: (1) every branch points at exactly one benchmarkSlug, never an average; (2) a branch may land on "no clean answer yet" and that is a valid, full-weight line; (3) where two legitimate metrics disagree (EQ-Bench Elo vs Rubric) the disagreement becomes TWO branches ("Best head-to-head reader preference -> EQ-Bench, ranked by Elo" and "Best rubric-scored craft -> EQ-Bench, ranked by Rubric, note gpt-5.5 leads Rubric yet trails on Elo"), never collapsed into one false winner. No price/speed/latency/context branch ever, because those require the locked-out model catalog.
proposal.honestPickDesign:
A pick is a claim welded to its single receipt, rendered as a PickStamp: a thin adaptation of the existing ScoreStamp atom (which must be EXPORTED from benchmark-detail-client, along with FlagChip, monYear, domainOf; today they are module-local). Layout, top to bottom, in a hairline-separated row (borderBottom var(--color-border-subtle), the LeaderRow idiom), NO ordinal badge medal:
- line A (the claim): a MicroLabel "BEST FOR MINIMIZING MADE-UP FACTS" (the pick label), in --color-text-3.
- line B (the verb, trust-gated): the named model + lab at 15px 600, prefixed by a verb that is a DETERMINISTIC function of the backing benchmark's trust tier, authored once in a PICK_VERB lookup mirroring TRUST_META: live -> "tops"; near_ceiling -> "leads (scores are saturated)"; contested / selfReported -> "claims the top of"; dated -> "led as of MON YYYY"; near-tie -> demotes to a co-leaders framing ("share the top of"); honest-empty -> NO pick line is emitted at all, the slot becomes the HonestEmpty body.
- inline FlagChip row (reused verbatim): Self-reported (steel) and/or Disputed (gold), plus a NEW Scaffold chip (gold tile, same FlagChip component, new label) when the tested unit is an agent scaffold not a bare model.
- line C: the verbatim score string + baselineGloss (kept exactly, intervals preserved), the benchmark name as an .editorial-link to /benchmarks/[slug].
- line D: monYear(asOfDate) + domainOf(sourceUrl) link, identical to ScoreStamp line 3.
- line E (caveat, structural not optional): one --color-gold-hued sentence stating the single caveat the pick rests on ("Board last readable April 2026, predates the newest models" or "Score belongs to the Pointer Agent scaffold running Opus 4.7, not Opus 4.7 alone, you cannot get this from the bare model"). A pick cannot render without this field populated.

WHAT A PICK MAY CLAIM: only "top of THIS one named board, on THIS date, by THIS metric", with its caveat attached. WHAT IT MAY NOT CLAIM: best model in general; any cross-benchmark or composite standing; any cost/speed/context superlative; any rank-order inside a near-tie (the near-tie note replaces the order); a scaffold score as a model score. Because the claim and all four receipts are one welded block, the flattering half (the model name) is physically un-screenshot-able without the qualifying half, which protects exactly the non-technical reader least able to add the caveat themselves.
proposal.honestEmptyHandling:
First-class, full-weight, never a faded fallback. Three distinct states, kept visually and verbally separate so they do not collapse into one dismissible grey:
1. HONEST-EMPTY use case (Long documents, Languages, optional Studying): the picks rail renders the PR1 HonestEmpty component verbatim, "No board here measures this cleanly, so we are not naming a winner", followed by the single most-cited dated anchor as a stone-tier line with the left-border treatment (LongBench v2: "human experts 53.7%, best direct model 50.1%, chance 25%"). The left column still teaches the full brief (which tests would measure this, why none is trustworthy: "the board is JS-rendered and unreadable" / "no independent leaderboard exists"). On the explorer, the row's col-3 reads a DATED chip + "No trustworthy current board", and the row sits in the grouped "Tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet" section. The gap IS the lesson, given parallel weight to a populated page.
2. SELF-REPORTED-ONLY (OmniDocBench): a pick IS emitted but its verb is gated to "claims the top of", it carries the Self-reported FlagChip, and its line-E caveat states the conflict verbatim ("self-reported in the README, and the #1 model shares a maintainer (OpenDataLab) with the benchmark"). Trust tier contested, gold styling.
3. DATED board (BFCL): pick verb is "led as of APR 2026", line E states "predates Opus 4.7/4.8, Gemini 3.1, GPT-5.5", and the backing chip on the explorer is the DATED stone chip. Past-tense framing throughout, never present-tense "the best tool-use model is X".

Empties are ordered into a labeled section, never hidden, so a student who opens Long context learns up front the evidence is sparse: no bait-and-switch.
proposal.dataModel:
USECASES TAG: add useCases String[] @default([]) to model Benchmark (schema.prisma ~line 550, beside relatedConcepts), add useCases: BenchmarkUseCase[] to the BenchmarkSeed type, and tag all 22 published-or-draft records. Add the read path: getBenchmarks already selects nothing extra; add useCases to the select and to BenchmarkCardData so the explorer can compute backing chips. A USE_CASE_SLUGS allowed-set + a BenchmarkUseCase union goes in seed-data, validated in scripts/seed-benchmarks.ts alongside the existing DOMAINS/TRUSTS checks (and run through the BANNED_DASHES regex).

USE-CASE CONTENT LAYER: a second Prisma model UseCase, mirroring Benchmark's draft-gate exactly (so it reuses the publish vocabulary, not a parallel invention):
model UseCase { id, slug @unique, name, audienceLine, taskLead (the bold framing sentence), whichTests (the trust-prose beat), orderedBenchmarkSlugs Json (string[]), howToChoose Json ([{ goal, benchmarkSlug, guidance }]), picks Json ([PickSeed]), watchOut String?, watchOutUrl String?, relatedConcepts Json?, honestEmpty Boolean @default(false), datedAnchor String?, status @default("draft"), createdAt, updatedAt, @@index([status]), @@map("use_cases") }. 
A PickSeed is { label, benchmarkSlug, model, lab, score (verbatim), baselineGloss, asOfDate, sourceUrl, selfReported, disputed?, scaffold? (bool), caveat (required) }. Picks are AUTHORED, never derived from leaders, so an author can cite one specific row and attach the exact caveat; the verb is computed at render from the backing benchmark's trust tier, not stored.

READ PATH: add lib/use-cases.ts mirroring lib/benchmarks.ts: UseCaseViewer reuse of getBenchmarkViewer; getUseCases(viewer) (where status published unless admin; ordering = live-backing-count desc then name, honest-empty last) returning UseCaseCardData { slug, name, audienceLine, backing: { slug, name, trust }[] } where backing is resolved by joining orderedBenchmarkSlugs against published benchmarks (drops drafts for members, like relatedConcepts validation); getUseCaseDetail(slug, viewer) returning the full content + resolved backing + parsed picks. Picks parsed defensively (asPicks, requires string model+caveat) the same way asLeaders works.

SEEDS: prisma/seed-data/use-cases.ts exporting USE_CASE_SEEDS, hand-authored from docs/research/benchmarks-usecases-leaders-extracted.md; plus the useCases tags added to BENCHMARK_SEEDS. A scripts/seed-use-cases.ts mirrors scripts/seed-benchmarks.ts (upsert by slug, --verify validates every benchmarkSlug exists and every picked slug is in orderedBenchmarkSlugs, BANNED_DASHES over all text fields, seeded as drafts).

ADMIN/PUBLISH: extend app/api/admin/benchmarks/route.ts is wrong scope; add app/api/admin/use-cases/route.ts as an exact copy of the benchmarks PATCH gate ({action: publish|unpublish, slug} and publishAll), reusing the same local requireAdmin. A UseCaseModeration card (copy of BenchmarkModeration) sits at the bottom of the use-case page for admins, hitting the new route.

fileMap.what: Add useCases String[] @default([]) to model Benchmark; add new model UseCase with the draft-gate shape (mirrors Benchmark.status). Run prisma db push + generate, then RESTART next dev on 3100 (HMR cannot reload .prisma/client).

New data layer: UseCaseCardData / UseCaseDetailData / UseCasePickView types, getUseCases(viewer), getUseCaseDetail(slug,viewer), asPicks defensive parser, backing-benchmark resolution against published rows. Reuses getBenchmarkViewer.
Add useCases to the getBenchmarks select and BenchmarkCardData so the explorer can derive backing chips.
Export CategoryTab (lift from module-local) so the hub toggle and any reuse share it. No other change to the PR1 list.

Export ScoreStamp, FlagChip, monYear, domainOf, and the prose primitives (Beat, Prose, MicroLabel, ExampleTask, BackLink, GoDeeper, WatchOut) so the use-case page reuses them instead of duplicating. Add a 'Scaffold' label path to FlagChip usage (gold tile).
New thin client: the two-mode ghost-text toggle (By use case | All benchmarks) rendering UseCaseExplorerClient or BenchmarksClient. Owns no list logic.
New: the single-column hairline explorer. UseCaseRow copied from BenchmarkRow grammar; col-3 evidence readout = StatusChip sm pills + honesty gloss; honest-empty group under a HairRule + label.

New: prose-led use-case page. Left = task lead + which-tests beat + how-to-choose branches + WatchOut + GoDeeper. Right = UseCasePickPanel with PickStamp lines (trust-gated PICK_VERB) and the PR1 HonestEmpty for empty cases. UseCaseModeration for admins.
Fetch both getBenchmarks and getUseCases, render BenchmarksHubClient. Keep force-dynamic + title 'Benchmarks | AISA Atlas'.
New route: await params, getUseCaseViewer/getUseCaseDetail, redirect('/benchmarks') on miss, render UseCaseDetailClient. force-dynamic, same metadata title.
New: USE_CASE_SEEDS (11 use cases) + the BenchmarkUseCase union and USE_CASE_SLUGS allowed-set. Authored from the leaders-extracted research; picks carry verbatim scores + required caveats.
Add useCases: [...] to every BenchmarkSeed record; extend BenchmarkSeed type with useCases.
New seeder mirroring seed-benchmarks.ts: upsert by slug as draft, --verify (every benchmarkSlug exists, every pick slug is in orderedBenchmarkSlugs), BANNED_DASHES over text fields.
Add USE_CASE_SLUGS validation for the new useCases tag on benchmark records.
New publish gate: PATCH publish|unpublish|publishAll over UseCase.status, copy of the benchmarks route with the same requireAdmin.

proposal.motionTokensA11y: Zero new chrome, zero new tokens. Motion: the explorer reuses the benchCellIn entrance + 18ms capped stagger + the .editorial-link sweep exactly as BenchmarksClient defines them (the <style> block is copied into the explorer component, same as PR1 copies it by value); the use-case page reuses .trend-detail-enter (benchDetailFade 260ms) and benchHeadlineIn. Every animation gated by usePrefersReducedMotion and the @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) block that sets animation-name:none, copied verbatim. Tokens: only the verified set: --maxw-content, --color-bg, --color-text / -2 / -3, --color-border / -subtle, --color-accent (editorial #4255FF via data-surface), --tile-indigo for ordinal badges, the four trust tile pairs via StatusChip, --color-gold + --color-gold-soft for the WatchOut bar / DraftChip / pick caveat line / Scaffold chip. No --shadow-card, no new card grid CSS, no --tile-slate. A11y: the full-row Link carries aria-label = use-case name; StatusChip carries the trust WORD (color is never the sole signal); the evidence gloss ("3 boards, 1 live") is real text, not a color cue; pick verbs are words, not icons; honest-empty states are authored prose, readable by screen readers as content, not as an empty region; tab order follows DOM order (toggle, then rows); the <=600px and <=720px collapses reuse the PR1 !important media rules so the rail stacks under the prose with no horizontal scroll.

Seeded as drafts.

No route renders yet.
