proposal.reuseVsNew: REUSE (verbatim or copied-by-value, the editorial convention): TRUST_META, StatusChip (sm), TRUST_TAB_ORDER, splitLead, usePrefersReducedMotion, SectionEyebrow, HairRule, ArrowRight, DraftChip, the tile-indigo ordinal badge, the .editorial-link sweep <style>, the benchCellIn entrance + stagger, the <=600px / <=720px collapse rules, the .trend-detail-split/.trend-detail-rule/.trend-detail-enter grid, and the full LeaderPanel honest-empty body. EXPORT-then-reuse (today module-local, no logic change): CategoryTab; ScoreStamp, FlagChip, monYear, domainOf; Beat, Prose, MicroLabel, ExampleTask, BackLink, GoDeeper, WatchOut. NEW (justified, minimal): UseCaseRow (a renamed BenchmarkRow with a chip-row col-3, no new CSS), PickStamp (ScoreStamp + a label line + a required gold caveat line), PICK_VERB lookup (a TRUST_META-shaped map gating the claim verb), the col-3 evidence readout, the Scaffold FlagChip label, the UseCase model + lib/use-cases.ts + the two new routes + seeds + seeder + admin route. NO new card system, NO repeat() grid, NO new color or shadow token: the explorer is hairline rows precisely because a card grid would be new chrome that betrays the cardless trust-first surface.
topRisks: Receipt drift: if PickStamp ever lets the bold model name render while the caveat sits faint or behind hover, the honesty becomes decorative. Mitigation: caveat is a REQUIRED field on PickSeed (seeder rejects empty) and line E is part of the welded block, not optional copy; the verb itself is trust-gated so a thin board literally cannot print a confident verb.

Backdoor composite: a 'tops N of 11' tally, a most-picked-model summary, or sorting the explorer by a model's win count would reintroduce the locked-out aggregate. Mitigation: explorer ordering is by live-backing count of the USE CASE (not any model), no model name appears on the explorer at all, and picks are authored per board with no cross-page rollup.

Scaffold-as-model confusion (OSWorld): a non-technical student reads 83.6% as a model they can call. Mitigation: a Scaffold FlagChip + a mandatory line-E disclosure ('not Opus 4.7 alone, you cannot get this from the bare model'); the verb stays gated to the board's tier.

Primitive export churn: exporting ScoreStamp/FlagChip/prose atoms from benchmark-detail-client could destabilize PR1 if signatures drift. Mitigation: export only, no signature change; PR2c is a separate reviewable PR with a Playwright pass over the unchanged /benchmarks/[slug] detail to prove no regression.

Two publish gates and a useCases tag create three sources of truth (benchmark status, use-case status, the tag). A use case could surface a draft-only benchmark or a stale chip. Mitigation: getUseCases resolves backing against PUBLISHED benchmarks only for members (the relatedConcepts validation pattern), so a draft benchmark never leaks a chip; --verify cross-checks every slug at seed time.

Honest-empty fatigue: with 6 empties and 2 self-reported, many pages look sparse and a student may read the whole surface as broken. Mitigation: empties are authored full-weight prose with a dated anchor and a labeled explorer section ('tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet'), so sparseness reads as intentional teaching, not a bug; the four states (live/dated/self-reported/empty) stay verbally and chromatically distinct.

Prisma client desync after adding the UseCase model (the known trap): routes 500 with 'cannot read undefined'. Mitigation: after db push + generate, kill and restart next dev on 3100; verify the title reads 'AISA Atlas' before bisecting any 404/500 as a code bug.

attacks.verdict: FIX, with one element to KILL: the repeat(2) card grid as the explorer idiom. Keep the page, the dated bottom line, the welded picks, and the scaffold chip; replace the grid with the hairline UseCaseRow.
attacks.trustIntegrity: Mostly clean: no composite, no overall score, no model catalog, picks are authored and welded to a single board, the dated bottom line is trust-gated and the four absence states stay distinct. One real leak: this is the only proposal that authors picks[] as standalone copy AND admits the duplication, so a stale pick is a live dishonesty vector. Its own risk register flags it but the mitigation is just convention (author to cite the same row) plus a future-PR promise to derive from leaders. That is weaker than proposal 4, which RESOLVES the receipt from Benchmark.leaders at render so the date and score can never drift. trustIntegrity is fine in intent but soft in mechanism.
attacks.audienceFit: Strong on its own terms: the benchlm idiom is the most recognizable to a mixed-background student and the dated bottom-line sentence is the single most decision-ready element across all four proposals. But the card grid actively works against trust literacy for THIS audience: eleven equal bordered cards in a 2-up grid read as ranked, comparable peers, which is exactly the false-comparison reflex the PR1 panel rejected one altitude down. A non-technical student is the reader MOST likely to misread a card wall of tasks as a leaderboard of tasks. The dated bottom line earns the keep; the grid undercuts the audience it claims to serve.
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Honest-empty is handled at full weight at three altitudes and the why-empty explanation plus dated anchor plus yardstick routing is genuinely first-class, which is correct given 6 empty plus 2 self-reported. But the card grid makes the COMMON case look worst: a 1-board-0-live card sitting in a tidy grid next to a 3-live card reads as a broken or unfinished cell, not as deliberate teaching. A hairline row that is simply shorter reads as intentional; a card with empty real estate reads as a hole. The grid amplifies honest-empty fatigue precisely where this roster cannot afford it.
attacks.buildability: Buildable on the stack, but it is the proposal that invents the most chrome. It adds a NEW .usecase-grid class with repeat(2,minmax(0,1fr)); the existing .browse-grid it cites as precedent is actually a flex column (globals.css line 336), NOT a repeat() card grid, so the claimed reuse is thinner than stated and this is a genuinely new grid system on the cardless editorial surface. Everything else (export ScoreStamp/FlagChip, UseCase model, admin route) is honest reuse. The new card CSS is the one buildability cost that violates the no-new-chrome rule without a strong enough justification.
attacks.scope: Honest 4-PR split (A data, B page, C explorer, D polish) with PR-A shipping no UI, which is reviewable. Authoring cost is the same as everyone (11 use cases). The duplication between picks[] and leaders adds a maintenance tax the others avoid. Scope is honestly stated; the grid is the only thing inflating it for no trust payoff.

attacks.verdict: FIX, with one element to KILL: repointing /benchmarks to the decision-flow explorer and demoting the PR1 Standings to a new /benchmarks/all route. Keep the decision-walk page, the trust-gated verbs, the judge-it-yourself honest-empty reframe.
attacks.trustIntegrity: The cleanest of the three on intent: no composite, the rail explicitly refuses to crown an overall winner (As of MON YYYY, not top model is X), picks are the exported ScoreStamp atom so the name cannot render without the receipt, verbs are trust-tier-gated, and metric disagreement becomes two branches. No leak I can find. This proposal and mine agree most on the trust spine. The only nit: storing leader value strings on the pick (value verbatim) instead of resolving from Benchmark.leaders reintroduces a small drift surface, though it does validate benchmarkSlug against live rows.
attacks.audienceFit: The how-to-choose-as-spine framing is genuinely the best decision scaffold of the four for a confused student: it decomposes the goal and routes each sub-goal to one board, which is the sanctioned escape from the locked-out composite. The judge-it-yourself-on-these-two-things reframe for honest-empty is the single best honest-empty move in any proposal and I am stealing it. The risk: making the LANDING page a bare question (What are you trying to do?) with no Standings in sight may strand the skeptical or benchmark-literate student who arrived wanting the trust index, now buried one route away.
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Best-in-class. The decision-aid reframe converts a dead end into a method, the dated anchor sits in the rail, the four states stay distinct, and the explorer row shows thinness up front. This is the proposal that most fully makes honest-empty a feature. No notes; keep all of it.
attacks.buildability: Fully buildable with PR1 primitives, single new CSS dependency is just reusing trend-detail-split and the bench-row collapse, no new card system, two small lookups (USE_CASE_SLUGS, VERB_BY_TRUST). The 5-PR split with a pure-export PR-C and a screenshot-diff acceptance on the unchanged brief is the most disciplined regression guard of any proposal. Buildability is excellent.
attacks.scope: This is the scope problem. Repointing /benchmarks and MOVING the PR1 list to /benchmarks/all is the largest front-end rework of the four, it changes the canonical URL of the already-built trust surface, and it risks relitigating the PR1 placement the brief told us not to redesign. The brief invites reworking surface PLACEMENT, but demoting The Standings to a secondary route is a bigger bet than the trust payoff requires when a two-mode toggle (my approach) keeps both doors at /benchmarks at a fraction of the churn and review surface. 5 PRs is honest but heavier than needed.

attacks.verdict: KEEP as the low-risk baseline, FIX two elements: stacking the explorer ABOVE the full Standings on one page (Option A), and rendering raw BenchmarkRow yardsticks inside a use-case page. KILL nothing on trust grounds; this is the safest proposal.
attacks.trustIntegrity: Strongest single mechanism of all four: getUseCaseDetail RESOLVES each pick receipt from the source benchmark's leaders Json at render, so a pick literally cannot drift from its board. That is the right answer to the duplication risk the other two carry, and I am adopting it. No composite, no catalog, no cross-use-case tally, verbs trust-gated, the no-model-name-on-the-explorer architecture enforces honest-picks-only structurally. Clean. The one thing to watch: rendering raw BenchmarkRow rows (with their own STATUS chip and four-word caveat) as the yardstick list is fine, but it must not turn the use-case page into a mini-leaderboard re-sequenced by score; ordering must stay trust-then-authored, never score.
attacks.audienceFit: Adequate but the weakest decision scaffold for the audience. Reusing BenchmarkRow as the yardstick list teaches the boards but does LESS to walk a confused student to a confident pick than proposal 3's how-to-choose spine or proposal 2's dated bottom line. Option A (explorer stacked above the entire 22-row Standings) produces a very long scroll where the task-first reader has to pass the whole trust index, which buries the decision the page exists to serve. The lens metaphor is honest but passive; a mixed-background student gets evidence, not a guided decision.
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Handled well: EmptyPick reuses the HonestEmpty left-border anchor inside the pick frame, thinness is visible on the explorer, the four states are distinct, self-reported is downgraded with a conflict caveat. Fully first-class. No notes here; this matches my own stance.
attacks.buildability: The most buildable and lowest-risk of the four, by design: zero new CSS, zero new tokens, one additive prop on BenchmarksClient, picks resolved from existing leaders, BenchmarkRow lifted by export. The one buildability snag it honestly flags: BenchmarkRow is module-local and must be exported or duplicated, and its bench-row keyframes must travel with it. Minor and stated. This is the reuse benchmark the others should be measured against.
attacks.scope: Smallest, most reviewable scope (PR2a data, PR2b explorer block, PR2c page, optional PR2d), and the only proposal that touches the PR1 trust index essentially zero. The honest scope cost: Option A's single long page is a UX compromise made for scope reasons, and the proposal itself offers Option B (a separate /benchmarks/use index) as the escape hatch, which is closer to my toggle. Scope is the strength; the front-end ambition is the deliberate sacrifice.

ideasToKeep: RESOLVE every pick's receipt from the source Benchmark.leaders Json at render time (reuse-maximalist) so the date and score can never drift from the board; this is the correct fix for the pick-duplication risk that benchlm-data-forward and decision-flow both carry, and it should be the canonical pick provenance for all stances.

The judge-it-yourself-on-these-two-things honest-empty reframe (decision-flow): when a use case is honest-empty, replace the picks with No board measures this cleanly, judge it yourself on these two things plus two authored plain-language criteria, with the dated anchor still in the rail. Converts a dead end into a method and is the strongest honest-empty payload of any proposal.

The dated bottom-line sentence (benchlm-data-forward), made honest and trust-gated: a single As of MON YYYY ... the one board we trust, but ... line at the top of every use-case page. The most decision-ready single element across all four; keep the sentence, drop the card grid it came packaged with.

Trust-tier-gated pick verbs via a small PICK_VERB / VERB_BY_TRUST lookup mirroring TRUST_META (all three): live tops, near_ceiling leads-but-tied, contested/selfReported claims, dated led-as-of, near-tie co-leaders. The claim's loudness must track the evidence tier deterministically, not be authored free-hand.

The SCAFFOLD FlagChip plus a mandatory what-was-tested line for OSWorld (all three): a non-technical student must not read 83.6% as a bare-model score; the scaffold-not-model disclosure is a structural field on the pick, not optional copy.

Metric-disagreement as two branches, never a false winner (all three): EQ-Bench Elo vs Rubric becomes two how-to-choose sub-goals (claude-fable-5 leads Elo, gpt-5.5 leads Rubric) so the order's dependence on the metric is surfaced, not hidden.

Per-goal how-to-choose as the sanctioned escape from the locked-out composite (decision-flow's spine, shared by all): decompose the goal, route each sub-goal to exactly one board, allow no-clean-answer-yet as a full-weight answer, split only on task shape a benchmark measures.

Visible honesty density on the explorer: show each use case's 2-3 backing benchmarks as their PR1 StatusChip BEFORE the click, so thinness (1 DATED chip) is a scannable warning, not a post-click disappointment. All four converge on this; it is correct.

A real UseCase Prisma model mirroring Benchmark's draft-gate plus admin publish route (all four), not a static map, so the use-case content layer inherits the publish discipline and the BANNED_DASHES seed validation for free.

Validate every benchmarkSlug on a use case against live published benchmarks at read time, reusing the relatedConcepts validation pattern (all four), so a draft or deleted board never leaks a chip or a receipt to a member.
ideasToKill: The repeat(2, minmax(0,1fr)) card-grid explorer (benchlm-data-forward's .usecase-grid). It is new chrome on a cardless editorial surface; the cited .browse-grid precedent is actually a flex column (globals.css line 336), not a card grid; and eleven equal cards silently imply the use cases are ranked, comparable peers, which is the exact false-comparison read the PR1 panel rejected one altitude down. The explorer must be a hairline UseCaseRow index, not a card wall.

Demoting the PR1 Standings to a new /benchmarks/all route and repointing /benchmarks to a bare question page (decision-flow). It is the largest churn of the four, changes the canonical URL of the already-built trust surface, and risks relitigating the PR1 placement the brief said not to redesign, when a two-mode toggle keeps both doors at /benchmarks for far less review surface.

Option A: stacking the explorer above the ENTIRE 22-row Standings on one page (reuse-maximalist). It buries the decision the page exists to serve under the full trust index; prefer the two-mode toggle or that proposal's own Option B (a separate use-case index).

Storing leader value strings on the pick as standalone authored copy (benchlm-data-forward's picks[] with admitted duplication; decision-flow's value verbatim field). Replace with render-time resolution from Benchmark.leaders. A stale pick is the exact dishonesty this page exists to flag, and a convention plus a future-PR promise is not a sufficient guard.

Any card hover that lifts a box, scales, or implies a clickable contestant tile, and any ordinal badge read as a RANK rather than a position (a risk in every card-leaning proposal). On a use-case surface, position must never read as quality.

Rendering the use-case page yardsticks as raw BenchmarkRow rows re-sequenced by score (a latent risk in reuse-maximalist). Yardstick order must be trust-then-authored, never score, or the page becomes the mini-leaderboard the spine forbids.
critique.bestExplorerIdiom: A single-column hairline index at --maxw-content built by copying the PR1 BenchmarkRow grammar verbatim into a UseCaseRow (tile-indigo position badge, use-case name as the .editorial-link hero at --text-xl, a plain-language audienceLine in --color-text-2, full-row absolute Link, inheriting the bench-row <=600px collapse and benchCellIn stagger for free), NOT a benchlm card grid. The right cell replaces PR1's single StatusChip with an evidence readout: the 2-3 backing benchmarks each wearing their size=sm StatusChip in TRUST_TAB_ORDER plus a computed honesty gloss (3 boards, 1 live or No trustworthy current board), so trust density is scannable before any click. No model name and no score ever appears on this surface, which enforces honest-picks-only at the architecture level. Honest-empty use cases sit at full weight in a quietly labeled trailing group (Tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet) under a HairRule, so sparseness reads as deliberate teaching. The explorer is one of two doors at /benchmarks via a single ghost-text toggle (By use case | All benchmarks) reusing the exported CategoryTab visual; the PR1 Standings stays at the same URL, completely unchanged internally. A row of equal cards reads as a league table of tasks; one hairline column reads as a contents page of doors, which is the only honest shape for non-comparable use cases.
critique.bestPickShape: A PickStamp: the exported PR1 ScoreStamp atom (the three welded lines that never separate: model + lab / verbatim score string + baseline gloss / monYear(asOfDate) + domainOf(sourceUrl)) with a claim label above and a required caveat line below, rendered as a hairline-separated row (borderBottom var(--color-border-subtle)), never a podium, never a vs-#N delta, never a medal. Line A: a MicroLabel claim (BEST FOR MINIMIZING MADE-UP FACTS) plus the backing board's size=sm StatusChip so STATUS is the hero of the pick exactly as it is the hero of every PR1 row. Line B: model + lab prefixed by a verb that is a DETERMINISTIC function of the backing benchmark's trust tier (PICK_VERB lookup: live tops, near_ceiling leads-scores-saturated, contested/selfReported claims-the-top-of, dated led-as-of-MON-YYYY, near-tie demotes to share-the-top-of), with inline SELF-REPORTED (steel) / DISPUTED (gold) / new SCAFFOLD (gold) FlagChips. Lines C-D: the verbatim ScoreStamp receipt, with the score and date RESOLVED at render from the source Benchmark.leaders Json so they can never drift from the board. Line E: one --color-gold-hued caveat sentence stating the single thing the pick rests on (board last readable April 2026; or score belongs to the Pointer Agent scaffold, not the bare model) which is a REQUIRED field the seeder rejects empty. What a pick MAY claim: only top of THIS one named board, on THIS date, by THIS metric, subject to THIS caveat. What it may NEVER claim: best model in general; any composite, average, or cross-benchmark standing; any tops-N-of-11 tally; any rank inside a near-tie; any price/speed/context superlative; a scaffold score as a model score. An honest-empty board emits NO pick line at all; the slot becomes the PR1 HonestEmpty body plus the dated anchor plus the judge-it-yourself criteria. Because the flattering half (the name) is physically un-screenshot-able without the qualifying half, the welded block protects exactly the non-technical reader least able to add the caveat themselves.

proposal.designLanguageName: The Receipts Wall (editorial card explorer + receipted picker)
proposal.oneLineThesis: Borrow benchlm's proven information design (a scannable use-case card grid, a dated bottom-line, decision-ready pick cards, a how-to-choose block) but weld a STATUS chip and a single-benchmark + date + caveat receipt onto every facet and every pick, so a mixed-background student gets the fast, recognizable surface they expect while the page makes over-trust physically impossible.
proposal.explorerSurface: An "Explore by use case" card grid added ABOVE the PR1 trust list on /benchmarks, built by adapting the validated Brilliant-style bordered browse-card aesthetic already proven in this codebase (border 1px var(--color-border) + var(--radius-2), NO --shadow-card, NO benchlm drop-shadow) so it stays inside the editorial voice and the no-new-chrome rule. The grid is a NEW CSS class .usecase-grid in app/globals.css: display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(2,minmax(0,1fr)); gap:16px (justified below; it is the one new class). It collapses to 1fr at <=720px reusing the same breakpoint the .trend-detail-split already uses. Section header is a SectionEyebrow "Pick by what you are doing" + an h2 "Explore by use case" + one 16px text-2 lead, with a top-right editorial-link "Browse all 22 benchmarks ->" (ArrowRight) that scrolls to the PR1 list anchor (mirrors benchlm's top-right "Browse all rankings", honest because it points at the same boards). Each CARD (a Link to /benchmarks/use/[slug]) carries, top to bottom: (1) the use-case NAME as hero at --text-xl 600 letterSpacing -0.02em (the student task in plain language, never a model name, never a score); (2) the one-line audienceLine in 14px text-2; (3) a hairline (1px var(--color-border-subtle), margin 14px 0); (4) the TRUST-MIX SIGNAL row: the 2 to 3 ordered backing benchmark names, each wearing a StatusChip size="sm" (verbatim PR1 primitive), so before any click the card shows that Coding has three sage LIVE chips while Long context shows one stone DATED chip; (5) a tiny 11px text-3 meta line "N boards, M live" using tabular-nums; (6) an ArrowRight bottom-right that translateX(3px) on the card-hover via the editorial-link svg rule. Card hover lifts only the border to var(--color-accent) and the name to var(--color-accent) (reuse the .bench-name color transition), NO shadow, NO scale. Cards are ordered by an editorial logic (most-live-backed first, honest-empty grouped last) and are NEVER numbered, so the grid reads as eleven doors not a league table of tasks. Honest-empty use cases (Long context, Languages, optional Studying) stay in the grid at full weight; their card shows the DATED/CONTESTED chips and the meta reads "1 board, 0 live" so thinness is a visible, honest signal at load, not a post-click surprise. Entrance reuses benchCellIn (the existing keyframe) with the same capped 18ms stagger and is gated by usePrefersReducedMotion.
proposal.explorerLivesWhere: The explorer is ADDITIVE to PR1, not a replacement: /benchmarks stays one route and one BenchmarksClient, gaining the .usecase-grid section between the header lead and the existing trust-filter tab row. The PR1 trust-first hairline list (The Standings) survives verbatim below the grid as the "all 22 benchmarks" foundation and gets an id anchor (id="all-benchmarks") so the explorer's "Browse all 22 benchmarks ->" link and the use-case pages' "see the full brief" links target it. What changes about PR1: (a) the page lead copy gains one sentence routing students to the grid ("Start from your task below, or read all 22 boards."); (b) the trust-filter tab row gets a small "All 22 benchmarks" SectionEyebrow above it to mark the boundary between the task-first grid and the board-first list; (c) BenchmarksClient now receives a useCases:UseCaseCardData[] prop alongside benchmarks. The trust spine, BenchmarkRow, StatusChip, the honest-empty state, and the sort order are untouched. The sidebar stays a single /benchmarks item: isActive is already a prefix match, so /benchmarks/use/* keeps it active with no new nav (confirmed components/sidebar.tsx lines 124-129). No sub-nav chrome is introduced.
proposal.useCasePageSurface: /benchmarks/use/[slug] is a NEW route (app/(main)/benchmarks/use/[slug]/page.tsx) mirroring the PR1 detail route exactly: export const dynamic="force-dynamic"; metadata title "Benchmarks | AISA Atlas"; params is a Promise (await params); awaits getUseCaseViewer-equivalent (reuse getBenchmarkViewer) then getUseCaseDetail(slug, viewer); if (!useCase) redirect('/benchmarks'). It renders a new UseCaseDetailClient under data-surface="editorial" inside the (main) data-theme="light" shell at --maxw-content, padding "40px 40px 96px", with the .trend-detail-enter fade. Layout top to bottom (single column, max prose width 660, this page is NOT a two-column split because the picks are the rail): (1) BackLink "Benchmarks" (copied from detail client) to /benchmarks. (2) Domain-style kicker eyebrow "USE CASE". (3) h1 = the use-case name (clamp 30-40px). (4) the audienceLine as the 19px 500 bus-stop lead. (5) THE DATED BOTTOM LINE, benchlm's signature move made honest: a single SectionEyebrow "Bottom line" + a callout block (left-border 3px, NOT a tinted box) whose sentence is trust-tier-gated and ALWAYS dated, e.g. "As of APR 2026, Claude Opus 4.5 leads BFCL V4, the one independent tool-use board we trust, but that board predates the newest models, so treat it as the last clean reading." For an honest-empty use case the bottom line instead reads "No board here measures this cleanly, so we are not naming a leader. Here is the yardstick and the most recent anchor." (6) HairRule. (7) "What this is" beat (Beat + Prose, copied primitives): the task framing in plain language. (8) "The yardsticks" beat: the ordered backing benchmarks as a hairline list, each row = StatusChip + benchmark name (editorial-link to /benchmarks/[slug]) + a one-line "what it reads" gloss + an inline "also central to Science" cross-tag note where shared; this is the goal-to-evidence routing that teaches the benchmark even when the leader is empty. (9) HairRule. (10) "Decision-ready picks" beat: a hairline-separated COLUMN of honest pick cards (never a podium, never a vs-#N delta), one per authored sub-goal, often one and sometimes zero. (11) "How to choose" beat: a per-goal branch list, one line per sub-goal each landing on exactly one benchmark or an honest "no clean answer yet". (12) WatchOut beat in gold (the one structural recolor) carrying the use-case-level caveat (metric disagreement, scaffold-not-model, freshness). (13) GoDeeper concept cross-links reusing the existing primitive. Picks and yardsticks both degrade to first-class honest-empty content, never a faded fallback.
proposal.howToChooseDesign: A "How to choose" beat that keeps benchlm's per-goal branch structure but strips every locked-out axis. It is a hairline-separated list; each branch is one row: a bold sub-goal question on line 1 (text, 15px 600) and the answer on line 2 (text-2) pointing at exactly ONE benchmark by name (editorial-link to its brief) plus its caveat. Branches split ONLY on task shape a benchmark actually measures, NEVER on price/speed/latency/context (those need the rejected catalog) and NEVER on a composite. Example for Factual accuracy: "Worried it invents citations? -> Watch SimpleQA (short-fact recall), but it is honest-empty right now, so no current leader." / "Need expert-level knowledge it will not fake? -> Watch Humanity's Last Exam, where today's leaders still score low, which is the honest signal." For Writing, the metric-disagreement case is surfaced as TWO branches rather than collapsed: "Want head-to-head reader preference? -> EQ-Bench ranked by Elo (claude-fable-5 leads)." / "Want graded craft on a rubric? -> the same board's Rubric score, where gpt-5.5 leads instead, which is why the order depends on which you trust." Each branch is authored content in the use-case layer (howToChoose: {goal, benchmarkSlug, answer}[]), validated dash-free, and renders a StatusChip size="sm" beside the named benchmark so the confidence of each branch is visible. A branch may resolve to "no clean answer yet" with the reason, which is a complete, honest answer.
proposal.honestPickDesign: The honest pick card reuses the WELDED ScoreStamp grammar (which must be EXPORTED from benchmark-detail-client.tsx, or lifted into benchmarks-client.tsx alongside the other shared primitives, plus FlagChip, monYear, domainOf) so a pick is physically unable to render its model name without its receipt. Card shape (a bordered row in the picks column, border-bottom 1px var(--color-border-subtle), NO box, NO medal, NO delta): LINE 1 the PICK LABEL as an eyebrow ("Best for minimizing hallucinations") in 11px 600 0.08em uppercase text-3; LINE 2 model + lab (15px 600 text); an inline FlagChip row when self-reported/disputed/scaffold (steel SELF-REPORTED, gold DISPUTED, and a NEW gold SCAFFOLD chip added to the FlagChip vocabulary for OSWorld); LINE 3 the verbatim score string + baselineGloss ("83.6% success, human baseline 72.36%"); LINE 4 the normalized "MON YYYY" date + source domain link (monYear + domainOf); LINE 5 the trust-tier-gated caveat in 11px text-2, gold-hued when it is a hard caveat. WHAT A PICK MAY CLAIM: only "top of THIS one named board on THIS date", phrased by a verb deterministically gated by the backing benchmark's STATUS tier (a small PICK_VERB map mirroring TRUST_META): live -> "leads / tops"; near_ceiling -> "leads, but the top is a statistical tie"; contested or selfReported -> "claims the top spot (self-reported)" + conflict caveat; dated -> "led as of MON YYYY"; near-tie -> demotes to "co-leads with X, within N points". WHAT A PICK MAY NEVER CLAIM: "best model" in general; any cross-benchmark or cross-use-case aggregate; a number averaged across rows; an order inside a near-tie band; a bare model name a student "can just use" when the tested unit is a scaffold (the SCAFFOLD chip + a "what was tested: Pointer Agent running Opus 4.7, not the bare model" line is mandatory for OSWorld). A use case emits AT MOST as many picks as its boards honestly support, frequently one, and when the only board is honest-empty it emits ZERO picks and the picks beat renders the honest-empty block instead.
proposal.honestEmptyHandling: Honest-empty is the EXPECTED, most common state and is rendered as full-weight authored content, never a faded or coming-soon stub. It appears at three altitudes, all reusing PR1 vocabulary: (1) EXPLORER CARD: an honest-empty use case keeps its full-size card with its DATED/CONTESTED StatusChips and a "1 board, 0 live" meta line, so thinness is a visible honesty signal at load. (2) BOTTOM LINE: instead of a leader sentence, "No board here measures this cleanly, so we are not naming a leader," then the single most-cited dated anchor (e.g. LongBench v2: "human experts 53.7%, best direct model 50.1%, chance 25%, as of the last readable snapshot"), reusing the HonestEmpty + datedAnchor treatment from the detail rail verbatim. (3) PICKS BEAT: emits zero pick cards and instead renders an authored explanation of WHY no number survived (JS-only board, no independent leaderboard, self-reported with a maintainer conflict), parallel in weight to a populated pick row, with the dated anchor as a left-border stone-tier line so the page still teaches calibration with zero leaders. The four states stay visually and verbally distinct to avoid caveat-fatigue greyness: truly-empty (stone, "no board"), dated (stone chip, "led as of"), self-reported (steel SELF-REPORTED chip + conflict caveat), scaffold (gold SCAFFOLD chip + "what was tested" line). OmniDocBench's self-reported top-3 is shown ONLY with the SELF-REPORTED chip AND the maintainer-conflict caveat welded to it, at lower visual confidence than an independent board, so the page never becomes the aggregator it exists to flag.

proposal.reuseVsNew: REUSE (verbatim, no fork): TRUST_META, TRUST_TAB_ORDER, StatusChip, splitLead, SectionEyebrow, HairRule, ArrowRight, DraftChip, usePrefersReducedMotion (already exported from benchmarks-client.tsx); the benchCellIn + benchDetailFade keyframes and the editorial-link/svg sweep <style> blocks; the .trend-detail-split/.trend-detail-rule breakpoint (<=720px); data-surface=editorial + --maxw-content; the relatedConcepts live-validation pattern; the BenchmarkModeration admin-card shape; the redirect-on-miss + force-dynamic route shape; getBenchmarkViewer. EXPORT-THEN-REUSE (currently module-local, must be exported in PR-B, no duplication): ScoreStamp, FlagChip, monYear, domainOf, Beat, Prose, MicroLabel, ExampleTask, WatchOut, GoDeeper, BackLink. NEW (justified): UseCase Prisma model + Benchmark.useCases (no read path exists today); use-cases.ts seed + use-case admin route (no use-case publish gate exists); UseCaseDetailClient + use-case-explorer (new surfaces); ONE new CSS class .usecase-grid (the editorial surface has no repeat() card grid; justified because eleven equal HONEST-EMPTY-tolerant cards are the benchlm information design the stance commits to, built from the validated Brilliant bordered-card aesthetic with no shadow so it stays in the editorial voice); a SCAFFOLD case added to FlagChip (the one genuinely new caveat type OSWorld introduces); a PICK_VERB map (mirrors TRUST_META, gates pick language by trust tier).
topRisks: The card grid is the single biggest deviation from PR1's cardless, league-table-averse spine. If the cards read as ranked or comparable, it reintroduces exactly the false-winner problem PR1 rejected. Mitigation: never number the cards, order by editorial logic not score, withhold all model names and scores from the card, and keep the chip-mix (not a number) as the only per-card signal.

Pick/leader data duplication: authoring picks[] separately from Benchmark.leaders means the two can drift, and a stale pick is exactly the dishonesty this page exists to prevent. Mitigation: author picks to cite the same row, validate asOfDate/source against the backing board in the seeder, and flag a future PR to derive picks from leaders.

Honest-empty dominates the roster (6 empty + 2 self-reported + SimpleQA/MMMU-Pro/Design2Code likely empty), so several use cases ship with zero picks. Risk: the page feels hollow to a student expecting answers. Mitigation: make the why-empty explanation + dated anchor + yardstick routing first-class content so an empty page still teaches the benchmark and calibration.

Scaffold-as-model confusion on the Computer use case: a non-technical student reads 83.6% as an Opus 4.7 score. Mitigation: mandatory SCAFFOLD chip + a what-was-tested line welded into the pick; no scaffold pick renders without it.

Self-reported laundering on OmniDocBench (number-1 shares a maintainer with the benchmark). Mitigation: SELF-REPORTED chip + an explicit conflict caveat on the pick, rendered at lower visual confidence than an independent board, trust=contested.

Scope: this is four PRs touching schema, seed, a new model, a new route, an admin gate, two new clients, and an export refactor. Risk of a large unreviewable diff. Mitigation: the PR-A/B/C/D split keeps the data foundation, the page, the explorer, and Playwright independently reviewable, and PR-A ships no UI.

Prisma schema change footgun (per memory): after db push/generate the dev server must be restarted or routes 500 with 'cannot read properties of undefined'; and Turbopack HMR desync after the export refactor can spuriously 404 the new route. Mitigation: restart dev + rm -rf .next after PR-A and after the PR-B export changes before verifying.

attacks.trustIntegrity: Strong, with one real leak. The architecture-level enforcement is the best of the four: a model name CANNOT appear on the explorer at all (col-3 is StatusChip pills only), and the PickStamp welds the verb to the trust tier via PICK_VERB, so a thin board literally cannot print a confident verb. No composite, no averaging, no catalog. The leak is the col-3 honesty gloss '3 boards, 1 live'. This is a derived numeric summary of a use case's evidence, and it is one rename away from being read as a use-case quality score. Worse, the explorer ORDERS rows by live-backing count, so the top row genuinely is 'the use case with the most live boards', which a non-technical student reads as 'the most answerable / most important task'. That is a soft composite over benchmarks, computed not authored. It does not invent a leader, but it ranks use cases by an evidence tally, which is exactly the move my stance worries about: a number that looks like a verdict. Fix: keep the gloss as words first ('most boards live' vs 'no live board'), and label the section split so the ordering reads as grouping (answerable vs sparse), not ranking.
attacks.audienceFit: Weakest of the three on first contact, strongest on depth. The thesis 'a use case is a door, not a contestant' is correct and protects the student. But the explorer surface gives a mixed-background student almost nothing recognizable: a hairline list of tasks with chip clusters, no shape they have seen on artificialanalysis or benchlm. The col-3 'evidence readout' (a row of sm StatusChip pills plus '3 boards, 1 live') is a literacy artifact that ASSUMES the student already understands what live/dated/contested mean, before the page has taught it. A first-time student does not parse a steel chip vs a stone chip as confidence; they parse it as decoration. benchlm earns trust by looking like a tool the student recognizes, then subverting it honestly. This proposal looks like an index the student must already be fluent to read. The use-case PAGE, by contrast, is excellent for the audience: the bold task lead, the which-tests beat, the eyebrow-over-prose how-to-choose, all land. The problem is purely the front door.
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Best in class. This is the proposal's real strength and it directly answers the common case. Three distinct, full-weight states (honest-empty, self-reported-only, dated) kept verbally and chromatically separate, the PR1 HonestEmpty body reused verbatim in the rail with equal slot weight, the empties grouped under an authored 'Tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet' label so sparseness reads as a deliberate section. The honest-empty page still teaches the full brief (which tests would measure this, why none is trustworthy). When 6 of 22 are empty and 2 self-reported, this is the design that does not collapse. My one note: grouping all empties LAST risks a student never scrolling to their task (Long context, Languages) and only seeing the populated top, but that is a minor ordering quibble against an otherwise correct treatment.
attacks.buildability: Highest buildability of the four, lowest risk. Verified against the codebase: every primitive it leans on is real, and its export-then-reuse list (CategoryTab from benchmarks-client; ScoreStamp, FlagChip, monYear, domainOf, Beat, Prose, BackLink, WatchOut, GoDeeper from benchmark-detail-client) is exactly the set that is module-local today, confirmed by grep. It adds ZERO new CSS classes (UseCaseRow reuses the bench-row grammar, the page reuses .trend-detail-split), which is the cleanest read on the no-new-chrome rule. The BenchmarksHub wrapper is a thin toggle with no list logic. The only buildability cost is the primitive-export churn risk it honestly names itself.
attacks.scope: Honest and well-phased. Three PRs (data foundation no-UI, explorer plus hub, page plus picks) with real Playwright acceptance criteria. The hub toggle is the one scope question: it makes 'By use case' the DEFAULT landing, which silently demotes the PR1 Standings to a second tab. That is a bigger product decision than the proposal admits (it changes what every existing /benchmarks link lands on), but it is reviewable and reversible. Authoring cost (11 use cases plus picks) is the same across all four and is acknowledged.
attacks.verdict: KEEP the use-case page, the PickStamp, PICK_VERB, the three-state honest-empty handling, and the no-new-CSS discipline. FIX the explorer front door: it is too austere and too literacy-assuming for a mixed-background student, and the live-backing-count ordering plus '3 boards, 1 live' gloss is a soft evidence-tally that flirts with a use-case ranking. KILL the default-to-use-case hub toggle as the silent demotion of The Standings; if a toggle ships, default to The Standings or make the choice explicit with James.

attacks.trustIntegrity: Strong and principled, no composite. The 'decompose the goal, the sub-goal selects the single board' framing is the correct, sanctioned escape hatch from averaging, and it is stated more sharply here than anywhere else. VERB_BY_TRUST gates the claim loudness; the rail's stamp is 'As of MON YYYY', explicitly NOT 'the top model is X', which kills the overall-winner crown. No model catalog, no cross-benchmark table. One subtle risk it shares with my own stance: the use-case page renders the backing benchmarks as 'the SAME BenchmarkRow rows re-ordered', and re-ordering rows by trust-then-authored-order inside a single task page can read as 'these benchmarks are ranked against each other for this task'. They are not comparable (an Elo board vs a pass-rate board), so the sequence must be presented as reading order, not a ranking. Mitigable, but it is the same league-table-by-sequence trap the explorer avoids.
attacks.audienceFit: Best framing of the four for the audience, with the biggest unbuilt promise. Opening on 'What are you trying to do?' instead of 'Explore by use case' is the single best audience move in any proposal: it meets a non-technical student exactly where they are. The decision-walk page (task, yardsticks, how-to-choose as the SPINE, watch-out, one receipted pick) is genuinely confidence-building. The honest-empty reframe ('we are not naming a winner, judge it yourself on these two things' plus two authored criteria) is the strongest single idea in the entire set for the audience: it converts a dead end into a method the student can actually use. The risk is that this proposal promises the most authored judgment (per-use-case judging criteria, decomposed sub-goals, metric-disagreement branches) and so depends most heavily on author quality; a thin or generic authoring pass would make the 'walk' feel like filler.
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Excellent, second only to editorial-minimalist on rigor and arguably ahead on usefulness. The 'judge it yourself on these two things' decision-aid is the most actionable honest-empty treatment proposed, and the rail still carries the PR1 HonestEmpty dated anchor so calibration is taught with zero invented numbers. Four distinct absence states. It holds up fully when a use case has zero live leaders, because the page's value (the method) does not depend on a leader existing. This is the right instinct for a roster that is mostly empty.
attacks.buildability: Buildable but the HIGHEST front-end-redo cost of the four, and it under-sells one risk. It is the only proposal that REPOINTS /benchmarks away from The Standings entirely (the explorer becomes the front door; the PR1 list moves to a NEW /benchmarks/all route). The brief invites reworking the surface, so this is permitted, but it means every existing inbound link, screenshot, and the sidebar's mental model now lands on the explorer, and The Standings becomes a click away. Verified: the sidebar prefix-match (pathname.startsWith(href + '/')) does keep /benchmarks/all and /benchmarks/use/* active, so that claim holds. The five-PR split is clean. But moving the canonical trust surface is a structural change dressed as a routing tweak.
attacks.scope: Five PRs, honestly the largest scope, but the most granularly reviewable (data tags, then UseCase model, then a pure export-refactor PR with a screenshot-diff acceptance, then explorer, then the walk). The pure-refactor PR-C with a pixel-diff gate is a genuinely good practice the others should copy. The authoring burden is the largest (judging criteria plus decomposed sub-goals plus metric branches per use case), and the proposal acknowledges drift risk and resolves it with live benchmarkSlug validation. The cost is honest; it is just high.
attacks.verdict: KEEP the 'What are you trying to do?' framing, how-to-choose as the SPINE not a sidebar, the 'judge it yourself on these two things' honest-empty decision-aid (the best idea in the set), VERB_BY_TRUST, and the pure-refactor PR with a screenshot-diff gate. FIX the use-case page's re-ordered BenchmarkRow yardsticks so a within-task sequence cannot read as a ranking of non-comparable boards (present as reading order, label it). FIX the repoint: moving The Standings to /benchmarks/all is a real product decision, surface it to James rather than shipping it inside a routing PR. KILL nothing; this is the strongest stance, it just costs the most.

attacks.trustIntegrity: Mostly strong, with the SINGLE most dangerous data-model choice in the set, and it is a TRUST risk not just a build risk. The honest-pick design is sound (HonestPick wraps the exported ScoreStamp, trust-gated PICK_VERB, no composite, no catalog). But the dataModel makes picks RESOLVE their receipt from the source benchmark's leaders Json at render time ('getUseCaseDetail RESOLVES each pick from that benchmark's actual leaders'). The intent (single source of truth, no drift) is good, BUT it collides head-on with the v2 data reality: the new-benchmark leaders (BFCL, OSWorld, OmniDocBench, EQ-Bench) are NOT all in Benchmark.leaders, and several are recovered, scaffold, or self-reported with caveats that do not live on the benchmark row. If a pick must resolve from leaders, then either (a) you cannot make a pick for a board whose leaders are honest-empty (correct), or (b) you are forced to stuff scaffold and self-reported leaders INTO Benchmark.leaders to make them resolvable, which would silently change what the PR1 brief renders and could surface a scaffold score AS a leader on the trust-first list. That is a trust regression on the spine the brief says not to touch. The authored-pick approach (editorial-minimalist, decision-flow, and my own stance) is safer precisely because the pick's caveat and scaffold note live on the pick, not on the board. Resolve-from-leaders is elegant and wrong for this roster.
attacks.audienceFit: Good, and the most recognizable-but-honest of the hairline approaches. Option A (explorer block ABOVE the untouched Standings on one page) is the most conservative answer and gives the student two reading orders without a mode toggle. The backing-chips-as-receipts strip on each explorer row is the same literacy-assuming risk as editorial-minimalist (chips before the page has taught the chips), but stacking the explorer directly above the familiar Standings means the student sees the trust vocabulary in both places, which helps. The use-case page reuses BenchmarkRow yardsticks, which is recognizable. Net: solid audience fit, not the boldest, not the worst.
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Good and explicitly first-class (EmptyPick reusing the HonestEmpty left-border anchor, four distinct states, thinness visible up front). It holds the common case. BUT it is undermined by the resolve-from-leaders model above: an honest-empty board has no leaders to resolve from, so the proposal must special-case empty:true picks with their own datedAnchor, which it does, but that means the 'single source of truth' claim only holds for the populated minority and the empty majority is authored anyway. So the elegant resolve mechanism buys little on a roster that is mostly empty, while adding the trust risk. The honest-empty rendering itself is fine; the data model that feeds it is the problem.
attacks.buildability: Genuinely the lowest NET-NEW-code claim, and the claim is mostly accurate, with one unacknowledged churn. It correctly identifies that almost everything is reusable. But it wants to LIFT/EXPORT BenchmarkRow to render the use-case yardsticks, and BenchmarkRow is module-local in benchmarks-client.tsx (confirmed by grep, line 222, not exported) AND it carries a bench-row <style> block and depends on BenchmarkCardData shape. Exporting it is fine, but reusing it on the use-case page means the use-case yardstick rows will render the FULL PR1 row including its own caveat and status column, which may be more than the page wants and couples the two surfaces tightly. The Option A choice to also schema-store Benchmark.useCases as Json? (vs String[]) to match the inline-Json convention is a reasonable, defensible call that the other proposals split on. Buildability is high; the BenchmarkRow coupling is underplayed.
attacks.scope: Honestly the smallest and most shippable, four PRs with an optional fourth. The 'ship value fast at lowest risk' framing is real and the phasing is clean. The scope honesty is its best quality. The only scope dishonesty is calling the BenchmarkRow lift 'a mechanical change' when it is a coupling decision.
attacks.verdict: KEEP Option A (explorer block above the untouched Standings, no mode toggle, no repoint), the HonestPick-wraps-ScoreStamp shape, the four distinct absence states, and the smallest-diff phasing discipline. FIX (really, KILL) the resolve-pick-receipt-from-leaders-at-render data model: on this mostly-empty, scaffold-and-self-reported roster it either forces nothing or forces polluting Benchmark.leaders with non-leader scores, which is a trust regression on the PR1 spine; author the pick receipt instead (with required caveat and scaffold fields), as the other three do. FIX the BenchmarkRow reuse: do not render full PR1 rows as use-case yardsticks; use a lighter StatusChip-plus-name-plus-gloss line so the two surfaces stay decoupled.

ideasToKeep: The 'What are you trying to do?' question as the explorer header (decision-flow-first): the single best audience move, meets a mixed-background student where they are, far better than 'Explore by use case'.

Honest-empty as a DECISION AID: 'we are not naming a winner, judge it yourself on these two things' plus two authored plain-language criteria (decision-flow-first): the most actionable empty-state treatment proposed; converts a dead end into a usable method, which is the right answer when 6 of 22 boards are empty.

Three-to-four distinct, verbally-and-chromatically-separate absence states (honest-empty / dated / self-reported-contested / scaffold) reusing the PR1 tier palette so the page never collapses into one dismissible grey (all three, sharpest in editorial-minimalist).

PICK_VERB / VERB_BY_TRUST: a TRUST_META-shaped lookup that gates the pick's verb deterministically by the backing board's trust tier (live tops / near_ceiling leads-but-tied / contested claims / dated led-as-of), so claim loudness tracks evidence and a thin board cannot print a confident verb (all three).

The welded PickStamp/HonestPick that wraps the exported ScoreStamp so the model name physically cannot render without its date, source, and flag chips (all three) plus a structural always-present caveat line and a new SCAFFOLD FlagChip for OSWorld.
Metric-disagreement rendered as TWO branches, never collapsed into a false winner (EQ-Bench Elo vs Rubric, where gpt-5.5 leads Rubric but trails Elo) (all three).

No model name ever appears on the explorer surface; the first time a model is named is inside a use-case page pick welded to its receipt, enforcing honest-picks-only at the architecture level (editorial-minimalist, reuse-maximalist).

Export-then-reuse with a dedicated PURE-REFACTOR PR gated by a Playwright screenshot-diff of the unchanged /benchmarks/[slug] brief (decision-flow-first PR-C): the right way to de-risk exporting ScoreStamp/FlagChip/BenchmarkRow without destabilizing PR1.

Option A placement: explorer as a block ABOVE the untouched Standings on the same /benchmarks page, no mode toggle, no repoint (reuse-maximalist): the lowest-risk way to add the explorer while leaving the trust spine and every existing inbound link exactly where they are.

Zero new CSS classes by reusing the bench-row grammar for explorer rows and .trend-detail-split for the page (editorial-minimalist): the cleanest read on the no-new-chrome rule; my own grid class is the one deviation and should be justified or dropped if a hairline list tests well.

A second Prisma UseCase model mirroring Benchmark's draft-gate plus inline-Json shape so it inherits the publish gate, the admin PATCH route, the DraftChip, and the BANNED_DASHES validation for free (all three).

Validate every pick/howToChoose/test benchmarkSlug against live published benchmarks at read time, exactly like relatedConcepts, so an unpublished or deleted board drops its receipt instead of leaking (all three).
ideasToKill: Resolve-pick-receipt-from-Benchmark.leaders-at-render-time (reuse-maximalist dataModel): on a roster that is mostly honest-empty plus scaffold plus self-reported, this either makes no picks or forces non-leader scores (scaffold, self-reported) INTO Benchmark.leaders to be resolvable, which would change what the PR1 brief renders and could surface a scaffold score AS a leader on the trust-first spine the brief locks. Author the pick receipt with required caveat and scaffold fields instead.

Ordering the explorer by live-backing COUNT and showing a derived '3 boards, 1 live' tally as the primary col-3 signal (editorial-minimalist): a computed evidence-tally that ranks use cases by board count reads to a non-technical student as ranking tasks by importance or answerability. Group answerable vs sparse with word-first labels, do not sort by a number.

Defaulting the /benchmarks landing to the use-case explorer via a mode toggle that silently demotes The Standings to a second tab (editorial-minimalist hub): changes what every existing inbound link lands on; if a toggle ships, default to The Standings or make the choice explicit with James.

Repointing /benchmarks to the explorer and moving the PR1 trust list to a new /benchmarks/all route (decision-flow-first): permitted by the brief but it relocates the canonical trust surface; this is a product decision for James, not a routing PR, and Option A (block above the untouched Standings) achieves the explorer with zero relocation.

Rendering full PR1 BenchmarkRow components as the use-case-page yardsticks (reuse-maximalist): couples the two surfaces and drags the full status column plus four-word caveat into a context that wants a lighter StatusChip-plus-name-plus-gloss line; use a lighter line so the surfaces stay decoupled.

ANY 'tops N of 11 use cases' tally, most-picked-model summary, single overall/composite score, AI-race crown, cross-benchmark mega-table, or Cheapest/Fastest/Largest-context decision card: violates the locked HONEST PICKS ONLY and no-model-catalog decisions. None of the four proposes these outright, but the soft evidence-tally and the live-backing-count sort are the nearest backdoors and must be watched.

Any how-to-choose branch that splits on budget, speed, latency, or context window: requires the locked-out model catalog. Branches split only on task shape a benchmark actually measures (all three correctly forbid this; keep it forbidden).
critique.bestExplorerIdiom: A single-column editorial HAIRLINE list of use-case rows (NOT a benchlm-style equal-card grid), placed as a block ABOVE the untouched PR1 Standings on the same /benchmarks page (reuse-maximalist Option A, no mode toggle, no repoint), opened with the decision-flow-first question header 'What are you trying to do?' instead of 'Explore by use case'. Each row reuses the BenchmarkRow grammar verbatim (tile-indigo position glyph, plain-language task name as hero, a one-line audienceLine, full-row Link to /benchmarks/use/[slug]) so eleven rows read as a contents page of doors, never a league table of eleven contestants. The right cell shows the 2-to-3 backing benchmarks as their PR1 StatusChips with short names (the honesty preview), but the per-row text signal is WORD-FIRST ('most boards live' vs 'no live board'), never a derived '3 of 3' count, and the surface is GROUPED into answerable vs 'tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet' rather than SORTED by a live-board tally, so thinness reads as a deliberate authored section and not a use-case ranking. No model name and no score ever appears on the explorer. This idiom is the synthesis: benchlm's scannable use-case entry point, in the PR1 cardless trust-first voice, with zero new CSS classes, zero repoint of The Standings, and no computed number that could be misread as a verdict.
critique.bestPickShape: An AUTHORED HonestPick card that wraps the exported PR1 ScoreStamp atom so the model name is physically un-renderable without its receipt, rendered as a hairline-separated row (borderBottom var(--color-border-subtle), NO box, NO medal, NO vs-#N delta), top to bottom: (line 0) a StatusChip of the backing board's trust tier PLUS the claim label 'Best for {sub-goal}' at 15px 600, so STATUS is the hero of the pick exactly as it is the hero of every PR1 row; (lines 1-3) the verbatim ScoreStamp three lines that never separate (model + lab / verbatim score string + baselineGloss / monYear(asOfDate) + domainOf(sourceUrl) link) with inline FlagChips; (the flag row) SELF-REPORTED steel, DISPUTED gold, and a NEW SCAFFOLD gold chip when the tested unit is an agent scaffold not a bare model; (final line) a STRUCTURAL, always-present caveat sentence in the gold caveat hue that the seeder rejects if empty, stating the single thing the pick rests on, plus a 'what was tested' clause for scaffolds ('Pointer Agent running Opus 4.7, not the bare model'). The receipt is AUTHORED on the pick, never resolved from Benchmark.leaders at render time, because the v2 roster's scaffold and self-reported scores must not be forced onto the PR1 trust spine. The verb is computed at render from a PICK_VERB lookup keyed to the backing board's trust tier (live tops / near_ceiling leads-but-the-top-is-tied / contested-or-selfReported claims-the-top-of / dated led-as-of-MON-YYYY / near-tie demotes to a co-leaders framing). A pick may claim ONLY 'top of THIS one named board, on THIS date, by THIS metric, subject to THIS caveat' and may NEVER claim best-model-overall, any composite or cross-benchmark standing, any rank inside a near-tie, any price/speed/context property, or a scaffold score as a bare-model score; and an honest-empty board emits ZERO picks, replaced by the PR1 HonestEmpty body plus the dated anchor at full slot weight. Strip the bold name and what remains is a true, dated, sourced, caveated sentence, which is the entire point.

proposal.designLanguageName: The Decision Walk (a guided, hairline-editorial decision path that reuses the PR1 trust spine: the page is a sequence of beats that walk a student from a task to the 2 to 3 yardsticks, to how far each can be trusted, to one receipted honest pick or a stated reason no honest pick exists; the same editorial voice, tokens, and welded ScoreStamp as PR1, with zero new chrome)
proposal.oneLineThesis: The /benchmarks v2 surface is not a catalog of tasks, it is a guided decision: pick your task, learn the 2 to 3 benchmarks that actually bear on it and how far to trust each, then read one honest pick welded to its single benchmark plus date plus caveat, or read plainly why nobody can honestly tell you yet and what to judge for yourself instead.
proposal.explorerSurface:
A SINGLE-COLUMN EDITORIAL HAIRLINE LIST, not a card grid. It reuses the PR1 BenchmarkRow grammar exactly (the bench-row pattern: tile-indigo ordinal-style lead glyph, name as hero at --text-xl, a one-line gloss in --color-text-2, an uppercase tracked meta strip, full-row absolute Link, the editorial-link sweep, the benchCellIn entrance with capped 18ms stagger, and the <=600px collapse). I deliberately reject benchlm's 4-column card wall: the PR1 panel already rejected a two-up grid for the index because a row of equal cells reads as a league table, and an explorer of 11 equal cards silently implies the use cases are ranked, comparable peers. A hairline column reads like a contents page (11 doors), not a scoreboard (11 contestants), and costs zero new chrome.

DECISION-FLOW FRAMING (my stance, the load-bearing difference from a plain explorer): the page does not open "Explore by use case." It opens with a question. Header: SectionEyebrow "Pick a model" + h1 "What are you trying to do?" + a 680px lead p ("Tell us the task. We will name the two or three tests that actually measure it, say how far you can trust each, and give you one honest pick with its date and its catch, or tell you plainly when nobody can."). The 11 use cases are framed as ANSWERS to that question, each labeled by the STUDENT TASK in plain language, never by benchmark name and never with a model name (a model name can never appear on the explorer because its date and caveat cannot fit, which enforces honest-picks-only at the architecture level).

EACH ROW (a UseCaseRow, a near-clone of BenchmarkRow):
- col 1 (36px): tile-indigo lead glyph. NOT a rank number: a small position dot or a single-letter task glyph, so it never reads as "use case #1 beats #2". Ordering is editorial (most-backed live first, honest-empty grouped last), explicitly never numbered as ranks.
- col 2 (1fr): task name as hero (--text-xl, the bench-name lift on row hover) e.g. "Coding and building apps", "Factual accuracy: not making things up", "Long documents and big context". Below it a one-line audienceLine in 14px --color-text-2 ("Build features, fix bugs, ship a small app"). Below that, instead of the DOMAIN . scoreType strip, an EVIDENCE STRIP: the 2 to 3 backing benchmark names each wearing a StatusChip at size="sm" (live | near ceiling | contested | dated), rendered inline. This is the row's honesty preview: a reader sees before clicking that Coding rests on three live boards while Long context rests on one DATED anchor.
- col 3 (172px, the bench-status slot): an EVIDENCE TALLY, not a trust chip for the use case itself (a use case has no single trust tier). It reads "3 live of 3" or, for thin cases, the words "No live board" in --color-text-2 with a DATED StatusChip. Honest-empty use cases are allowed to look thin here, and that thinness is the warning, never hidden.

A "Browse all 22 benchmarks ->" editorial-link sits top right of the header (mirrors benchlm's "Browse all rankings", routes to the PR1 list at /benchmarks/all), so the trust-first index stays one click away and is never destroyed.

No dynamic filter UI: 11 authored items is a knowable set, a faceted-search panel would imply an open queryable dataset and clash with the editorial voice and the no-new-chrome rule. (If a filter is ever wanted, lift the existing module-local CategoryTab; do not invent one.)
proposal.explorerLivesWhere:
The explorer BECOMES the front door of /benchmarks; the PR1 trust-first list is preserved and relocated, not destroyed. This is a rework of the PR1 surface placement, which the brief explicitly invites, while keeping the PR1 trust spine intact.

- /benchmarks (the index route, app/(main)/benchmarks/page.tsx) now renders the DECISION-FLOW EXPLORER ("What are you trying to do?") as the primary surface. This matches my stance: the durable product is the guided decision, so the decision question is the landing.
- /benchmarks/all (NEW route, app/(main)/benchmarks/all/page.tsx) renders the existing BenchmarksClient verbatim, unchanged: the full trust-first hairline index of all 22 benchmarks with the trust-axis CategoryTab filter. The PR1 list is the foundation a curious or skeptical student drops into; the explorer's "Browse all 22 benchmarks ->" link and every benchmark name on a use-case page route here / to a [slug]. Nothing about the PR1 list's trust spine, chips, ScoreStamp, or honest-empty state changes.
- /benchmarks/[slug] (the per-benchmark brief) is UNCHANGED. It remains the canonical, deepest trust artifact. Use-case pages link INTO it ("Read the full brief ->") rather than duplicating it.

What changes about PR1: only the routing and the page-level metadata/title of the two index routes; the BenchmarksClient component and benchmark-detail-client are untouched except that ScoreStamp, FlagChip, monYear, and domainOf get EXPORTED (they are module-local today) so the use-case picks reuse the welded receipt rather than forking it. No PR1 visual decision is relitigated. Sidebar keeps the single /benchmarks item (isActive is a prefix match, so /benchmarks/use/* and /benchmarks/all stay active); no sub-nav is added, the in-page "Browse all" and "Back to tasks" links carry navigation.
proposal.useCasePageSurface:
/benchmarks/use/[slug] is a DECISION WALK rendered as the PR1 two-column editorial split (.trend-detail-split + .trend-detail-rule, collapsing to one column and hiding the rule at <=720px), wrapped in data-surface="editorial" inside the (main) data-theme="light" shell, max-width --maxw-content, padding 40px 40px 96px, with the .trend-detail-enter fade. It mirrors benchmark-detail-client.tsx's structure beat-for-beat so it inherits the voice for free.

LEFT COLUMN (the decision spine, the prose: minmax(0, 1.5fr)). My stance puts "how to choose" as the SPINE here, not a sidebar:
1. BackLink: arrow-left "Back to tasks" -> /benchmarks (copy of the PR1 BackLink, retargeted).
2. Task kicker: an uppercase tracked label "DECISION" (the SectionEyebrow vocabulary) + a DraftChip when the use-case content is unpublished and viewer isAdmin.
3. h1 = the task in plain language ("Coding and building apps"), same clamp(30px,3.4vw,40px) as the benchmark h1.
4. One bold lead sentence (19px, 500, --color-text), the audienceLine expanded ("You want a model that can write, fix, and ship real code, not just toy snippets.").
5. HairRule, then BEAT 1 "The tests that matter" (Beat = SectionEyebrow + children, copied from detail-client): one Prose paragraph naming the 2 to 3 ordered benchmarks and what each adds ("Coding ability is read mainly through SWE-bench Verified, with LiveCodeBench and HumanEval as cross-checks"), followed by a YARDSTICK LIST: each benchmark as one hairline-separated row (borderBottom --color-border-subtle, the LeaderRow rhythm) carrying its name as an editorial-link to /benchmarks/[slug], a StatusChip size="sm", and the one-line "how far to trust it" gloss (pulled from TRUST_META.gloss or authored per use case). This is the literacy payload: the student leaves knowing the yardsticks even if no leader is nameable.
6. HairRule, then BEAT 2 "How to choose" (THE SPINE, full weight, not a sidebar): a per-goal branch list (HowToChoose primitive). Each branch is one line: a sub-goal in --color-text bold, an ArrowRight, then either a receipted PickCard inline (see honestPickDesign) or the words "no clean answer yet" with a one-line reason. Branches split ONLY on task shape a benchmark measures (e.g. "Minimizing hallucinations -> ... ; Knowledge-heavy Q&A -> ..."); NO budget / fastest / biggest-context branches (those need the locked-out model catalog). Where two legitimate metrics disagree, both framings appear as different sub-goals (EQ-Bench's Elo vs Rubric split) rather than collapsing into a false order.
7. HairRule, then BEAT 3 "What to watch", the ONE structural recolor: the WatchOut primitive (gold eyebrow + 3px gold bar) carrying the use-case-level caveat ("Two of these three boards are saturated; a high score no longer separates the top models" or "The only board here is dated to April 2026").
8. HairRule, then GoDeeper: concept cross-links validated against the live catalog, reusing the relatedConcepts vocabulary and the editorial-link sweep.

RIGHT RAIL (minmax(0, 1fr)): the DECISION SUMMARY, the analog of the LeaderPanel.
- A dated bottom-line stamp on a hairline: "As of MON YYYY" (the newest asOfDate across the use case's picks), uppercased via monYear, tabular-nums. NOT "the top model is X": the rail never crowns a single overall winner.
- The honest PickCards stacked, hairline-separated (PanelFooter rhythm), never a 1-2-3 podium and never a "vs #N" delta. Often one card; sometimes zero.
- A "What changed" micro-note when a board moved recently (optional, authored), in --color-text-3.
- PanelFooter analog: a line summarizing the strongest evidence tier present ("Best evidence here: one LIVE board" or "No live board for this task") + a "Browse all 22 benchmarks ->" editorial-link.

HONEST-EMPTY USE-CASE PAGE (e.g. Long documents and big context, Languages and translation): SAME geometry, full weight, never faded or "coming soon". BEAT 1 still names the yardstick and its DATED/honest-empty StatusChip. BEAT 2 "How to choose" is REFRAMED as a decision aid per my stance: instead of picks it reads "No board here measures this cleanly, so we are not naming a winner. Judge it yourself on these two things:" followed by 2 authored, plain-language judging criteria. The right rail replaces PickCards with the PR1 HonestEmpty block (the dated anchor with the left-border, e.g. "human experts 53.7 percent, best direct model 50.1 percent, chance 25 percent") so the page still teaches calibration with zero invented numbers. The absence is the lesson, rendered as deliberate content.
proposal.howToChooseDesign:
"How to choose" is the SPINE of the left column (BEAT 2), not a sidebar, per the decision-flow stance. It is a HowToChoose primitive: a vertical, hairline-separated list of per-goal branches. Structure of one branch:
- line 1: the sub-goal as a plain-language question or phrase, --color-text, 15px 600 ("Minimizing hallucinations on short facts").
- an ArrowRight (the PR1 inline SVG) as the "leads to" connector.
- line 2: the landing, which is EITHER an inline PickCard (the welded receipt, see honestPickDesign) OR, when the sub-goal's board is honest-empty, the literal phrase "No clean answer yet" in --color-text-2 plus a one-line reason in --color-text-3 ("no independent board measures short-fact accuracy that survived our check").

Hard rules baked into the primitive:
- Branches split ONLY on task shape a benchmark actually measures. NO "on a budget", "fastest", "biggest context" branches: those require the rejected price/speed/context catalog and are killed on sight.
- The number of branches is bounded by the use case's real benchmarks; a thin use case may have one branch or zero, and that is shown honestly, never padded.
- Metric disagreement becomes two branches, not a hidden choice: for Writing, "Best on head-to-head preference (Elo)" and "Best on rubric-scored craft" are separate branches landing on different picks, surfacing that the order depends on which metric you trust.
- The verb in each branch is trust-tier-gated (see honestPickDesign's verb lookup), so a branch off a DATED board reads "led the April board" not "is best".

This is the sanctioned escape hatch from the locked-out composite: instead of averaging non-comparable boards into one fake number, the goal is decomposed and the student's specific sub-goal selects the single board.
proposal.honestPickDesign:
CARD SHAPE: a PickCard is the welded ScoreStamp receipt with a claim label on top. It REUSES the exported ScoreStamp atom (newly exported from benchmark-detail-client.tsx) so a pick physically cannot render its name without its date, source, and flag chips traveling with it. Layout, top to bottom, never separable:
- line 0 (the claim label): "Best for {sub-goal}" in 13px 600 --color-text (e.g. "Best for minimizing hallucinations"). This is the ONLY new line above the standard ScoreStamp.
- then the verbatim ScoreStamp three lines: line 1 model + lab; the inline FlagChip row (Self-reported tile=steel, Disputed tile=gold) when present; line 2 verbatim score string + baselineGloss; line 3 monYear(asOfDate) + domainOf(sourceUrl) link.
- a CAVEAT line in --color-text-2 13px directly beneath, ALWAYS present, structural not optional ("Board predates the newest models, treat as the April reading.").
- when the tested unit is not a bare model, a SCAFFOLD disclosure line in the gold caveat hue (extends the FlagChip vocabulary with a SCAFFOLD chip alongside Self-reported/Disputed): "Led by a scaffold (Pointer Agent) running Opus 4.7; you cannot get this score from the bare model."

WHAT A PICK MAY CLAIM: only "top of THIS one named benchmark, on THIS date, by THIS verbatim score, subject to THIS caveat." It cites exactly one benchmark, one date, one score string, and the chip(s) that benchmark's trust state demands.

WHAT A PICK MAY NEVER CLAIM: "best model" in general; any cross-benchmark average, composite, or "overall"; any "tops N of M use cases" tally; any rank delta vs another pick; any price/speed/latency/context property. No pick may exist without a named benchmark, a date, and a caveat. No pick may be emitted at all from an honest-empty board (the slot becomes "no trustworthy leader yet" + the judging criteria instead).

CONFIDENCE GRADING (the pick's verb is a deterministic function of the backing benchmark's trust tier, authored once in a small VERB_BY_TRUST lookup mirroring TRUST_META, so the claim's loudness tracks the evidence):
- live -> "leads" / "tops".
- near_ceiling -> "leads, but the top is a statistical tie" (carry the PR1 near-tie note; demote a single name to a co-leaders framing when within noise).
- contested or selfReported -> "claims the top spot" + the conflict/self-reported chip and caveat (e.g. OmniDocBench's #1 shares a maintainer with the benchmark: SELF-REPORTED chip + an explicit conflict caveat).
- dated -> "led as of MON YYYY" in past tense + the DATED stone styling (BFCL April 2026).
A near-tie at the top always demotes a single-winner pick to a co-leaders framing (EQ-Bench #1/#2 within 12.5 Elo: shown as "effectively tied at the top", not #1 over #2).

CITATION MECHANICS: the date is monYear(asOfDate); the source is domainOf(sourceUrl) as an underlined link; the flags are the same FlagChip pills as PR1; the verbatim score is never reformatted. Because the whole unit is the ScoreStamp, you cannot screenshot the flattering half (the model name) without the qualifying half (date, self-reported, scaffold). The receipt is functional, not decorative.
proposal.honestEmptyHandling:
Honest-empty is a FEATURE and, given the data reality (6 of 22 honest-empty, 2 self-reported-flagged), the most common and most on-thesis state. It is handled at three altitudes, all reusing PR1, all full-weight:

1. EXPLORER ROW: an honest-empty use case stays in the list at full weight (never filtered out, never faded). Its evidence strip shows the backing benchmark with a DATED or contested StatusChip; its col-3 tally reads "No live board" in --color-text-2. The thinness is the scannable warning, set before the click, so the page is never a bait-and-switch.

2. USE-CASE PAGE (decision-flow reframe, my stance's signature move): the page keeps full geometry. BEAT 1 still names the yardstick and shows its honest-empty/DATED chip. BEAT 2 "How to choose" is REFRAMED from picks into a DECISION AID: "No board here measures this cleanly, so we are not naming a winner. Judge it yourself on these two things:" + 2 authored plain-language criteria (e.g. for Long context: "1. Does it keep facts straight across a long document, or start contradicting itself? 2. Does accuracy fall off a cliff past your real document length?"). This converts the dead end into actionable literacy: the student leaves with a method, not a name.

3. RIGHT RAIL: the PR1 HonestEmpty primitive verbatim: "No reliable current leaderboard. The most cited figure is a dated anchor:" + the single datedAnchor in the left-border block (human / best-direct / chance), so calibration is taught with zero invented numbers. PanelFooter reads "No live board for this task" + the StatusChip.

The four absence states stay VISUALLY AND VERBALLY DISTINCT (no uniform grey): honest-empty -> HonestEmpty block + dated anchor; dated -> DATED stone chip + past-tense "led as of"; self-reported -> Self-reported steel chip + conflict caveat; scaffold -> gold SCAFFOLD chip + "what was tested" line. Caveat fatigue is avoided by keeping each state's word and color from the PR1 palette distinct.
