proposal.reuseVsNew:
REUSE (verbatim or copied-by-value, no redesign): TRUST_META, StatusChip, SectionEyebrow, HairRule, ArrowRight, DraftChip, splitLead, usePrefersReducedMotion (already exported from benchmarks-client.tsx); the BenchmarkRow grammar + the bench-row <style> block (entrance, name-lift, <=600px collapse) for the explorer rows; the .trend-detail-split/.trend-detail-rule two-column shell + .trend-detail-enter fade for the walk; the Beat/Prose/MicroLabel/ExampleTask/WatchOut/GoDeeper/BackLink prose primitives (copied from benchmark-detail-client.tsx, the editorial-surface convention is copy-by-value); the LeaderPanel/HonestEmpty/PanelFooter rhythm for the right rail; the near-tie note copy; getBenchmarks/getBenchmarkDetail visibility + relatedConcepts-validation pattern; the admin route shape; the seed-script + BANNED_DASHES guard; all verified tokens and the data-surface=editorial wrapper.

NEWLY EXPORTED (was module-local, now shared so picks reuse the welded receipt, not a fork): ScoreStamp, FlagChip, monYear, domainOf from benchmark-detail-client.tsx, plus a SCAFFOLD FlagChip variant (reuses the steel tile).

GENUINELY NEW (justified against the editorial voice): (1) model UseCase + its seed/script/read-path/admin route, because no use-case content layer exists and the picks must be AUTHORED, never computed; built to mirror Benchmark's publish-gate shape so it inherits the trust/admin discipline. (2) three route segments (/benchmarks explorer repoint, /benchmarks/all, /benchmarks/use/[slug]), each cloning an existing route. (3) two client components (use-cases-client, use-case-detail-client) assembled almost entirely from reused primitives. (4) two small lookups: USE_CASE_SLUGS (validation) and VERB_BY_TRUST (trust-gated pick verb, mirrors TRUST_META). NO new card system, NO new color token, NO new chrome, NO shadow: the explorer is hairline rows (a card grid was explicitly rejected as a league-table read), and the walk is the existing split.
topRisks: Receipt drift: a PickCard could let the bold model name read loud while the date/caveat/chip fade. Mitigation: the pick IS the exported ScoreStamp atom (three lines that never separate) + a structural always-present caveat line; Playwright asserts every PickCard contains a MON YYYY token and a source link.

Composite leaking back: cross-use-case aggregation, a 'tops N of 11' tally, or the rail crowning one overall winner. Mitigation: no surface ever sums picks; the rail's stamp is 'As of MON YYYY' not 'top model is X'; picks cite exactly one benchmark; a Playwright check asserts no 'overall'/composite element.

Honest-empty read as broken: 6+ thin use cases could look like errors or coming-soon stubs to a non-technical student. Mitigation: full-weight geometry, the decision-aid reframe ('judge it yourself on these two things'), the dated anchor in the rail, and visible thinness on the explorer set before the click; never faded, never hidden.

Self-reported / scaffold laundering: OmniDocBench's conflicted #1 or OSWorld's Pointer-Agent scaffold shown at the same confidence as an independent board. Mitigation: trust-gated verbs (VERB_BY_TRUST), a mandatory conflict caveat, the SELF-REPORTED chip, and a SCAFFOLD disclosure line in the gold hue welded to the pick.
Stale presented as current: BFCL's April 2026 board read as 'is best' today. Mitigation: dated -> past-tense 'led as of MON YYYY' + the DATED stone chip, never present tense.

Prisma/Next gotchas in this fork: schema change without a dev restart 500s (per memory), and this Next variant awaits params + uses force-dynamic + redirect-on-miss. Mitigation: restart dev after every db push/generate; clone the existing route file shape exactly; verify on port 3100 with the 'AISA Atlas' title.

Authoring burden + drift: 11 authored use cases with picks must stay in sync as benchmark leaders move; a deleted/unpublished benchmark could orphan a pick. Mitigation: getUseCaseDetail validates every benchmarkSlug against live prisma.benchmark (drops orphaned receipts, like relatedConcepts today) and the seed script fails on unknown slugs; picks are dated so staleness is visible, not silent.

attacks.trustIntegrity: Clean on the big locks: no composite, no catalog, no mega-table, no fabricated leader. The col-3 evidence readout (a row of StatusChip pills plus the gloss '3 boards, 1 live') is honest because it counts the USE CASE's backing boards, not any model's wins, so it cannot become a backdoor 'tops N' tally. One subtle leak to watch: the trust-gated PICK_VERB is good, but 'leads (scores are saturated)' for near_ceiling still asserts a single name where my stance demotes to co-leaders. That is a verb-table fix, not a structural breach. Verdict on integrity: strong, near-identical to mine.
attacks.audienceFit: This is the weakest axis and the real reason it is not the best pick. It is the most disciplined and most editorially pure proposal, but it leads with 'By use case' as a noun-label index, not a question. A mixed-background student who does not yet know they are choosing a benchmark sees eleven nouns and an evidence-chip readout they cannot yet parse ('what is NEAR CEILING, why does that help me pick'). The literacy payoff is real but lands AFTER the click, in prose. My decision-flow framing ('What are you trying to do?') front-loads the student's own verb and makes the chips legible as an answer to a question they just asked. Keep the surface; borrow the interrogative header.
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Best-in-class handling, essentially co-equal with mine. Three distinct states (empty / dated / self-reported) kept chromatically separate, full-weight HonestEmpty body, the grouped 'Tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet' section with a quiet label. This is the strongest honest-empty treatment of the four and I would adopt the grouped-section idea wholesale. One gap: it does NOT convert the empty page into an actionable method for the student. The rail teaches calibration with the dated anchor, but the left column still only names yardsticks. My 'judge it yourself on these two things' reframe gives the non-technical student a takeaway when no leader exists; this proposal leaves them with a yardstick and no method.
attacks.buildability: Highest buildability of the three I am attacking. Pure reuse, one new component family (UseCaseRow, PickStamp, PICK_VERB), zero new CSS class, zero new token, copy-by-value of the bench-row style block which matches the established editorial convention. The hub-toggle rework is minimal and keeps BenchmarksClient internals untouched (only exports CategoryTab). The export-then-reuse of ScoreStamp/FlagChip/monYear/domainOf is confirmed feasible: they are module-local in benchmark-detail-client.tsx today. No invented chrome. This is the cleanest build plan in the set.
attacks.scope: Honest and tight: three reviewable PRs (data, explorer+hub, page+picks), data PR ships no UI, Playwright criteria are concrete. The one scope cost it understates: the two-mode hub toggle changes the DEFAULT landing of /benchmarks to 'By use case', which is a product decision with the same blast radius as my route split but dressed as smaller. It is fine, but it is not free.
attacks.verdict: KEEP as the structural baseline; this is the closest proposal to my stance and the safest to build. FIX two things: (1) replace the noun header 'By use case' with the interrogative decision-flow header so the audience gets a verb to grab; (2) add the honest-empty 'judge it yourself' decision-aid to the left column so an empty page yields a method, not just a yardstick. KILL nothing. The PickStamp welded block, the evidence readout, and the grouped-empties section are all keepers.

attacks.trustIntegrity: Holds the hard locks (no composite number, no catalog, no mega-table, authored picks). But it carries the highest trust-integrity RISK of the four because it commits to a 2-column card grid as the explorer. A grid of equal bordered cards is precisely the league-table read the PR1 panel rejected one level down. The author knows this and mitigates with 'never number the cards, order by editorial logic, withhold model names and scores.' That mitigation is necessary but not sufficient: equal-weight cards in a grid still imply the use cases are comparable peers, and a card's trust-mix chip row reads as a per-card score even without a number. The integrity is defended by author discipline, not by the architecture. My hairline column makes the same honesty structurally instead of by promise.
attacks.audienceFit: Best raw audience fit on first contact: the dated bottom-line sentence, the 'Bottom line' callout, the decision-ready pick cards, and the per-goal 'how to choose' are the most recognizable, fastest-to-decision surface for a non-technical student. The single-column use-case page with the dated bottom line is genuinely excellent and I would keep the dated-bottom-line move verbatim. The cost is that the recognizability is borrowed from benchlm's CONFIDENT tone, and confidence is the exact thing this page must withhold when 6 of 22 boards are empty. The surface promises decisions it often cannot honestly deliver.
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Strong on the page (zero picks, why-empty explanation, dated anchor, full-weight bottom line that swaps the leader sentence for 'no board measures this cleanly'). This is good and survives the common case. The FAILURE point is the explorer card: a card whose body is just two DATED chips and '1 board, 0 live' in a grid built for decisive cards reads as a broken or stub card, not as deliberate teaching. The empty state degrades worse in a card grid than in a hairline row, because a hairline row is naturally allowed to be sparse and a card visually advertises that it should be full. With empties as the COMMON case, the card idiom fights the data.
attacks.buildability: Buildable, but it is the only proposal that adds a new CSS class (.usecase-grid, repeat(2,minmax(0,1fr))), and that is a real concession against the no-new-chrome rule. The author justifies it via the validated Brilliant browse-card aesthetic with no shadow, which is a fair argument, but it is still net-new chrome on the cardless editorial surface, and it is the single biggest deviation from PR1's spine. Everything else (export ScoreStamp, copy BenchmarkModeration, the route shape) is sound reuse. Buildable, but the chrome cost is highest here.
attacks.scope: Four PRs (data, page, explorer, polish+Playwright), honestly scoped and independently reviewable, the data PR ships no UI. The placement (explorer ABOVE the unchanged PR1 list on one route, with an #all-benchmarks anchor) is the lowest-route-churn option and is a genuine merit. The understated cost is that stacking a card grid above the full 22-row trust list makes /benchmarks very long, which the author flags as a risk but does not resolve.
attacks.verdict: FIX, leaning KILL on the explorer idiom specifically. KILL the 2-column .usecase-grid card explorer: it reintroduces the league-table read the PR1 panel already rejected, adds new chrome, and degrades worst in the honest-empty common case. KEEP and port to a hairline surface: the dated 'Bottom line' callout (the single best use-case-page move in the whole set), the per-goal 'how to choose' branch list, the 'yardsticks' hairline list with per-board trust glosses, and the SCAFFOLD FlagChip. The page-level content design here is the strongest; only its explorer container is wrong.

attacks.trustIntegrity: Holds all the hard locks and adds one genuinely superior idea that I rank above every other proposal's data design: honestPicks RESOLVE their receipt from the source benchmark's leaders Json at render time rather than re-authoring the score. That single decision removes the largest trust-drift risk (an authored pick silently diverging from its board) and makes the receipt provably consistent with the cited benchmark. This is the best trust-integrity mechanism in the set and should be adopted regardless of which explorer wins. No composite, no catalog, no tally.
attacks.audienceFit: Weakest audience fit of the four, by its own design philosophy. The use-case page deliberately renders the actual BenchmarkRow rows (with their four-word caveats and STATUS chips) as the 'yardsticks' section. That is maximal reuse but it dumps the PR1 trust-list register onto a student who came to decide, not to read a board index. A mixed-background student gets a re-sorted benchmark list plus picks, which is closer to 'here are the boards' than to 'here is your decision'. It teaches the spine well; it guides the decision least. The explorer also omits the audience's verb (Option A is a labeled block 'Pick by task', not a question).
attacks.honestEmptyReality: Good and honest: EmptyPick reusing the HonestEmpty left-border anchor inside the HonestPick frame, four distinct states, thinness shown on the explorer chips. Survives the common case. But like the editorial-minimalist it stops at naming yardsticks and showing the anchor; it does not hand the student a method when no leader exists. And because the use-case page leans on rendering real BenchmarkRows, an honest-empty use case shows a DATED BenchmarkRow plus an EmptyPick, which doubles the 'nothing here' read without converting it into actionable guidance.
attacks.buildability: Highest pure reuse, lowest new-code count, explicitly the safest-to-ship. Reuses BenchmarkRow directly (must export it), wraps the exported ScoreStamp in HonestPick, copies BenchmarkModeration. The render-time pick resolution is slightly more logic in getUseCaseDetail but removes an entire class of drift bugs, a good trade. The Option A block above the trust list is the lowest route churn. No new token, no new card class, no new motion. Genuinely the cheapest correct build.
attacks.scope: Most honestly scoped and most reviewable: PR2a data, PR2b explorer block, PR2c page, PR2d optional Studying. The data PR ships no UI. The Json-column choice for Benchmark.useCases (vs String[]) matches the existing inline-Json convention and is the right call on this connector. Scope is the strongest axis here. One understated cost: lifting/exporting BenchmarkRow couples the use-case page to PR1 row internals, so a future PR1 row change can ripple into the use-case page, which the other proposals (that clone the grammar into UseCaseRow) avoid.
attacks.verdict: KEEP the data mechanism, FIX the page. KEEP, as the single best idea to adopt globally: render-time pick-receipt resolution from the source benchmark's leaders, so picks cannot drift. KEEP the hairline explorer block and the low-churn placement. FIX the use-case page: do NOT render raw BenchmarkRows as the body; that is reuse for reuse's sake and it buries the decision under a board list. Replace with a lean yardsticks hairline list (StatusChip plus a one-line trust gloss plus a brief link), which gives the literacy without the register clash. KILL nothing structural; the only thing to kill is 'BenchmarkRow as the use-case-page body'.

ideasToKeep: Single-column editorial HAIRLINE explorer (not a card grid): the contents-page idiom that three of four proposals independently converge on, because a row of equal cards reads as a ranked league table of tasks, the exact read the PR1 panel rejected.

Front the explorer with the student's VERB as a question ('What are you trying to do?') rather than a noun label ('By use case'), so a mixed-background student grabs a handle before meeting any trust vocabulary (decision-flow-first).

The welded PickStamp / HonestPick: export the module-local ScoreStamp, FlagChip, monYear, domainOf from benchmark-detail-client.tsx and wrap them so a model name physically cannot render without its date, source, and flag chips, plus a required caveat line.

RESOLVE each pick's receipt from the source benchmark's leaders Json at render time (reuse-maximalist) instead of re-authoring the score, so a pick can never drift out of sync with its board; adopt this regardless of explorer idiom.

Trust-tier-gated pick verb (a PICK_VERB / VERB_BY_TRUST lookup mirroring TRUST_META): live tops, near_ceiling demotes to co-leaders, contested/self-reported 'claims the top', dated 'led as of MON YYYY' past tense.

The dated 'Bottom line' callout on the use-case page (benchlm-data-forward) as a left-border line, never a tinted box, always dated, swapping to 'no board measures this cleanly' for empties: the single best use-case-page move in the set.

Per-goal 'How to choose' as the spine: branches split ONLY on task shape a benchmark measures, each landing on exactly one benchmark or an honest 'no clean answer yet'; metric disagreement (EQ-Bench Elo vs Rubric) becomes TWO branches, never one false winner.

Honest-empty as full-weight authored content with the four states kept visually and verbally distinct (truly-empty / dated / self-reported-contested / scaffold), plus a grouped 'Tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet' section on the explorer (editorial-minimalist).
The SCAFFOLD FlagChip plus a mandatory 'what was tested' line for OSWorld, so a student never reads an agent-scaffold score as a bare-model score.

Evidence readout on the explorer row: backing-benchmark StatusChips plus a plain-text honesty gloss ('3 boards, 1 live' / 'No live board'), so thinness is a scannable signal set BEFORE the click, never a post-click disappointment.

UseCase as a real Prisma model mirroring Benchmark's draft-gate and inline-Json shape (not a static map, not a Json blob on another model), so it inherits the exact admin publish path, the @@index([status]) visibility rule, and the BANNED_DASHES seed validation for free.
getUseCaseDetail validates every benchmarkSlug against live published benchmarks (the relatedConcepts pattern), so a draft or deleted board drops its chip/receipt instead of leaking.

Reuse-only motion and tokens: benchCellIn entrance with capped 18ms stagger, .trend-detail-enter fade, the editorial-link sweep, all gated by usePrefersReducedMotion plus the prefers-reduced-motion media block; zero new color or shadow token.
ideasToKill: The 2-column .usecase-grid card-wall explorer (benchlm-data-forward): reintroduces the league-table read the PR1 panel explicitly rejected, adds net-new chrome on the cardless editorial surface, and degrades WORST in the honest-empty common case where a card advertises it should be full.
Any new CSS card-grid class (repeat(2,minmax(0,1fr))) on the editorial surface: violates the no-new-chrome lock; the explorer must be hairline rows.

Rendering raw BenchmarkRow as the body of the use-case page (reuse-maximalist): reuse for its own sake that buries the decision under a re-sorted board index and couples the page to PR1 row internals; replace with a lean yardsticks hairline list.

Any 'tops N of 11' / most-picked-model / win-count summary or sorting the explorer by a model's wins: a backdoor composite; the explorer must order by the USE CASE's live-backing count and name no model at all.

Any overall/composite score, unified cross-benchmark mega-table, or model catalog field (price, speed, latency, context window) and therefore any Cheapest / Fastest / Largest-context card or 'on a budget' / 'fastest' how-to-choose branch: all locked out, kill on sight.
Crowning a single overall winner in the right rail: the rail's stamp is 'As of MON YYYY', never 'the top model is X'.
Present-tense framing of a dated board (BFCL April 2026 read as 'is the best tool-use model'): must be past-tense 'led as of APR 2026' with the DATED stone chip.

Showing OmniDocBench's self-reported, maintainer-conflicted #1 at the same visual confidence as an independent board: must carry the SELF-REPORTED chip plus an explicit conflict caveat at contested-tier styling.
A near_ceiling pick verb that still names one winner ('leads (scores are saturated)'): inside the noise it must demote to a co-leaders framing, never assert #1 over #2.
critique.bestExplorerIdiom: A single-column editorial HAIRLINE index at --maxw-content, built by cloning the PR1 BenchmarkRow grammar into a UseCaseRow (no new card class, no new token), and headed by the decision-flow QUESTION 'What are you trying to do?' rather than a noun label. Each row: a tile-indigo position glyph (never a rank), the use-case title as hero in plain student-task language, a one-line audienceLine, and in the right cell an evidence readout (the 2 to 3 backing benchmarks as size-sm StatusChips plus a plain-text honesty gloss like '3 boards, 1 live' or 'No live board'). No model name and no score ever appears on the explorer, which structurally enforces honest-picks-only (the first time any model is named is inside a use-case page, welded to its date and caveat). Rows are ordered editorially, most-live-backed first, with honest-empty use cases grouped last under a quiet HairRule and the label 'Tasks no benchmark measures cleanly yet', so thinness reads as deliberate teaching set before the click. A top-right 'Browse all 22 benchmarks ->' editorial-link preserves the PR1 trust-first Standings one click away. This wins because the hairline column makes the league-table avoidance STRUCTURAL rather than a matter of author discipline (the card-grid's fatal flaw), and it degrades gracefully in the honest-empty common case where a sparse hairline row is natural and a sparse card looks broken.
critique.bestPickShape: A PickStamp: the welded PR1 ScoreStamp atom (exported from benchmark-detail-client.tsx) with one claim label on top and one required caveat line beneath, rendered as a hairline-separated row (borderBottom var(--color-border-subtle)), NO box, NO medal, NO vs-#N delta. Top to bottom: (line 0) the claim label 'Best for {sub-goal}' preceded by a size-sm StatusChip of the backing board's trust tier, so STATUS is the hero of the pick exactly as it is the hero of every PR1 row; (lines 1 to 3) the verbatim ScoreStamp three lines that never separate, model + lab / verbatim score string + baseline gloss / monYear(asOfDate) + domainOf(sourceUrl) link, with inline SELF-REPORTED, DISPUTED, and the new SCAFFOLD FlagChips; (line E) a structural, always-present caveat sentence in the gold hue stating the single caveat the pick rests on, plus a mandatory 'what was tested' clause when the unit is an agent scaffold not a bare model. The leading VERB is a deterministic function of the backing benchmark's trust tier (a PICK_VERB lookup mirroring TRUST_META): live 'tops'; near_ceiling demotes to a co-leaders framing inside the noise; contested or self-reported 'claims the top of' plus the conflict caveat; dated 'led as of MON YYYY' in past tense. The pick's score/date/flags RESOLVE at render time from the source benchmark's leaders Json (reuse-maximalist's mechanism), so the receipt cannot drift from the cited board. A pick MAY claim only 'top of THIS one named board, on THIS date, by THIS metric, subject to THIS caveat'; it may NEVER claim best-model-in-general, any cross-benchmark average or composite, any cross-use-case tally, any order inside a near-tie, any price/speed/context superlative, or a scaffold score as a model score. When the only backing board is honest-empty, NO pick line is emitted: the slot renders the PR1 HonestEmpty body plus the dated anchor and (decision-flow-first) an authored 'judge it yourself on these two things' method, so an empty page yields a takeaway, not a hole. Because the flattering half (the model name) is one welded block with the qualifying half, it is physically un-screenshot-able without its caveat, which protects the non-technical reader least able to add the caveat themselves.

proposal.designLanguageName: The Standings, with Lenses (PR1 editorial hairline surface, unchanged; a thin use-case overlay layered on top)
proposal.oneLineThesis: Do not redo the front end: keep PR1's proven trust-first hairline index exactly as it is, and add the use-case explorer as one new contents-page row block above the existing list plus a /benchmarks/use/[slug] page that is mostly the SAME BenchmarkRow components re-ordered for one task, capped with honest picks rendered from the already-built welded ScoreStamp atom; the only genuinely new code is one exported HonestPick card and a tiny content layer, so we ship value fast at the lowest possible risk.
proposal.explorerSurface:
A single-column editorial HAIRLINE index of use cases, not a benchlm card wall. It reuses the PR1 BenchmarkRow grammar verbatim (tile-indigo lead glyph for position only, name as hero at --text-xl, a one-line audienceLine in --color-text-2, an uppercase tracked meta strip), so a row of eleven use cases reads like a contents page (eleven doors) and never a league table (eleven contestants). The PR1 panel already rejected a two-up grid for the index for exactly this reason; the explorer carries the same risk one altitude up, so it gets the same answer and zero new chrome.

Structure of one explorer row (component UseCaseRow, a near-copy of BenchmarkRow):
- Lead glyph: tile-indigo ordinal badge, position only (never a rank). Same 28px pill, same --tile-indigo-bg / -fg.
- Hero: use-case title, plain student-task language ("Factual accuracy: not making things up"), at --text-xl 600, with the .editorial-link bench-name sweep on row hover (full-row absolute Link to /benchmarks/use/[slug]).
- audienceLine: one sentence in --color-text-2, 14px, ellipsised on one line ("Pick a model that admits what it does not know instead of bluffing.").
- BACKING-CHIPS-AS-RECEIPTS strip (the one honest addition vs BenchmarkRow): in place of the single STATUS column, the right cell renders the 2 to 3 backing benchmarks as their PR1 StatusChip at size sm, each chip preceded by the bench short name in 11px text-3. So before any click the reader sees "Coding" carries three sage LIVE chips while "Long documents" carries one stone DATED chip. Honesty density is visible up front; thinness is a scannable warning, not a post-click disappointment. Reuses StatusChip verbatim.

Header block above the rows: reuse SectionEyebrow ("Pick by task") + an h1 ("Explore by use case") + a 680px lead paragraph in the PR1 header style, and a top-right .editorial-link reading "Browse all 22 benchmarks ->" (ArrowRight) that scrolls to / links to The Standings. No model name and no score appears anywhere on the explorer: the first time a model is ever named is inside a use-case page honest pick, welded to its date and caveat, which enforces honest-picks-only at the architecture level (there is no surface where a bare best-model string can leak).

Row order is editorial, never ranked: most-live-backed use cases first, honest-empty use cases grouped last, never numbered as quality. The optional "Studying and tutoring" honest-empty use case sits at the very bottom as the closing lesson if James green-lights it.

Responsive: inherits the PR1 .bench-row <=600px collapse for free (the backing-chips cell moves under the text via the same !important media rule, copied into the explorer component's own <style> block).
proposal.explorerLivesWhere:
The explorer is a thin overlay, NOT a replacement: PR1's /benchmarks ("The Standings") stays the canonical surface and the sidebar's single /benchmarks item is unchanged (its prefix-match isActive already lights up for /benchmarks/use/*, confirmed in components/sidebar.tsx lines 124-129, so no new nav item and no sub-nav are built).

Two reuse-maximalist placement options, recommend A:
- OPTION A (recommended, smallest change): the use-case explorer becomes a compact block ABOVE the existing trust list ON THE SAME /benchmarks page. BenchmarksClient gains one new section rendered before the trust-filter tabs: SectionEyebrow "Pick by task" + the eleven UseCaseRow hairline rows, then a HairRule, then the existing "State of the models" header and the untouched trust index below. One page, two reading orders (by task, or by trust), zero route churn, and The Standings spine is literally not edited. The page.tsx server route additionally calls a new getUseCases(viewer) and passes useCases into BenchmarksClient.
- OPTION B (if the explorer should be its own surface): a new index route app/(main)/benchmarks/use/page.tsx rendering a UseCaseExplorerClient with the same rows, and /benchmarks stays pure PR1. This adds a route but still touches no PR1 component.

What changes about PR1: essentially nothing structural. BenchmarksClient gets one additive prop (useCases) and one new section above its existing content in Option A; in Option B it is untouched. TRUST_META, StatusChip, splitLead, SectionEyebrow, HairRule, ArrowRight, DraftChip, usePrefersReducedMotion, the editorial-link sweep, the tile-indigo badge, and the whole single-column trust index are reused exactly as built. No token is added, no card system is introduced, the trust-first spine is not relitigated.
proposal.useCasePageSurface:
Route: app/(main)/benchmarks/use/[slug]/page.tsx, mirroring app/(main)/benchmarks/[slug]/page.tsx exactly: export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"; export const metadata = { title: "Benchmarks | AISA Atlas" } (title stays "... | AISA Atlas"); params is a Promise (await params); the server component awaits getBenchmarkViewer() then getUseCaseDetail(slug, viewer); if (!useCase) redirect("/benchmarks") so unknown slugs and member-invisible drafts both bounce. It passes { useCase, isAdmin } to a new UseCaseDetailClient.

The page is NOT a new layout: it is a thin assembly of PR1 primitives in three stacked editorial sections inside the data-surface="editorial" wrapper at --maxw-content, with the .trend-detail-enter fade. It deliberately does NOT use the two-column trend-detail-split (a leader rail per task would re-imply a league table); instead it is a single editorial column so each benchmark's evidence stays welded to its own context.

Section 1, TASK FRAMING (reuses BackLink, SectionEyebrow, the PR1 h1 + bold-lead pattern, Prose):
- BackLink-style arrow-left to /benchmarks.
- SectionEyebrow "Pick by task" + h1 = the use-case title.
- One bold bus-stop lead (19px 500) = the audienceLine expanded: what the task is in plain language.
- A "The evidence you are standing on" line (Goal-to-Evidence Routing): one Prose sentence naming the ordered backing benchmarks BEFORE any leader appears ("Coding ability is read mainly through SWE-bench Verified, with LiveCodeBench and HumanEval as cross-checks."), each bench name an .editorial-link to its /benchmarks/[slug] brief. The durable takeaway is the yardstick, so this lands even when every leader is empty.

Section 2, THE BENCHMARKS FOR THIS TASK (pure reuse, the heart of the reuse stance): a HairRule, SectionEyebrow "The yardsticks", then the SAME BenchmarkRow rows from PR1, re-ordered for this task (trust order then authored order within the use case), rendered by importing/lifting BenchmarkRow. Each row keeps its STATUS chip, four-word caveat, and links to the full brief. The use-case page is therefore mostly the existing list, filtered and re-sequenced. A small cross-link note under a shared benchmark uses the PR1 sweep ("also central to Science") for cross-tagging shown as honest shared membership, never a "tops N use cases" tally.

Section 3, HONEST PICKS (the one new component, HonestPick, see honestPickDesign): a HairRule, SectionEyebrow "Decision-ready picks", then a hairline-separated COLUMN of HonestPick cards (never a 1-2-3 podium, never a vs-#N delta), one per authored sub-goal, each welded to its single benchmark + date + caveat. Where the board is honest-empty the slot renders an honest-empty pick (see honestEmptyHandling) at full weight. Below the picks, a "How to choose" block (see howToChooseDesign).

Section 4, optional GoDeeper cross-links reusing the PR1 GoDeeper primitive to the related concepts for the task.

Admin: at the foot, a UseCaseModeration card (a near-copy of BenchmarkModeration) toggling the use-case content row's publish status via PATCH /api/admin/use-cases.
proposal.howToChooseDesign:
A short per-goal decision list (Per-Goal Disambiguation), the sanctioned escape hatch from the locked-out composite: instead of averaging non-comparable boards into one fake winner, keep them separate and let the student's specific sub-goal select the board. Rendered under SectionEyebrow "How to choose" as a hairline-separated list, one line per sub-goal, copy-grade plain language:

- Each line = a sub-goal phrased as a question + an arrow (ArrowRight) + exactly one benchmark to watch + its trust posture. Example for Factual accuracy: "Want short, checkable facts? -> watch SimpleQA (no current independent board, treat the paper anchor as the ceiling)." "Want knowledge-heavy expert Q&A? -> watch Humanity's Last Exam (live, but near the noise floor at the top)."
- Branches split ONLY on task shape a benchmark actually measures. No "on a budget", "fastest", or "biggest context" branches, because those need the rejected price/speed/context catalog. This keeps how-to-choose inside honest-picks-only and the no-model-catalog rule.
- Metric disagreement becomes two sub-goals, not a false ranking (Metric-Disagreement As First-Class). For Writing: "Care about head-to-head preference? -> EQ-Bench Elo (claude-fable-5 leads)." "Care about rubric craft? -> EQ-Bench Rubric (gpt-5.5 leads, and it trails on Elo, so the order depends on which you trust)." We surface BOTH framings rather than collapsing them.
- Each sub-goal line is authored content in the use-case content layer (howToChoose: { goal, benchmarkSlug, guidance }[]), validated so its benchmarkSlug exists and is one of the use case's backing benchmarks.
- Data structures and tokens only: text-2 body, ArrowRight, the editorial-link sweep on each benchmark name, gold hue reserved for any caveat clause. No new chrome.
proposal.honestPickDesign:
HonestPick is the single new exported component, and it is a deliberate re-skin of the PR1 welded ScoreStamp atom so the pick physically inherits the trust spine: you cannot render the flattering half (the model name) without the qualifying half (date, source, self-reported/disputed/scaffold chips). To reuse it, ScoreStamp, FlagChip, monYear, and domainOf (today module-local in benchmark-detail-client.tsx) are EXPORTED in a small PR2a refactor (no behavior change), and HonestPick wraps ScoreStamp.

Card shape (a hairline-separated row, borderBottom var(--color-border-subtle), NO box, NO shadow, NO medal, NO vs-#N delta):
- Line A, THE CLAIM LABEL: a sub-goal label at 15px 600 text ("Best for minimizing made-up facts") preceded by a small StatusChip(size sm) of the backing board's trust tier. The chip placement makes STATUS the hero of the pick exactly as it is the hero of every PR1 row.
- Line B-D, THE WELDED RECEIPT (the exported ScoreStamp, verbatim three lines): model + lab / verbatim score string + baseline gloss / monYear(asOfDate) + source domain link, with inline SELF-REPORTED and DISPUTED FlagChips. The score stays verbatim; only the date is normalized to "MON YYYY".
- Line E, THE PICK CAVEAT (one sentence, in the gold caveat hue family for emphasis when it is a real warning): the single caveat the pick rests on, phrased by trust tier (see trust-tier-gated verb below). A new SCAFFOLD FlagChip (tile=gold, sharing the Disputed hue) plus a one-line "what was tested" clause appears when the tested unit is not a bare model (OSWorld's Pointer Agent on Opus 4.7: "Led by a scaffold, Pointer Agent running Opus 4.7; you cannot get this score from the bare model.").

WHAT A PICK MAY CLAIM: only "top of THIS one named board, on THIS date, by THIS metric", and only as loud as the board's trust tier allows. The verb is a deterministic function of trust state (a tiny PICK_VERB lookup beside TRUST_META): live -> "tops" / "leads"; near_ceiling -> "leads, but the top is inside the noise"; contested or self-reported -> "claims the top spot" + conflict caveat; dated -> "led as of MON YYYY"; near-tie -> demote a single name to a co-leaders framing reusing the PR1 near-tie line.

WHAT A PICK MAY NOT CLAIM: never "best model" in general; never a number averaged across rows; never a pick without a named benchmark, a date, and a caveat; never a cross-use-case tally ("tops N of 11"); never a rank order inside a near-tie band; never present a scaffold score as a bare-model score; never present a dated board in crisp present tense.

A pick is the literal text of James's locked decision ("Best for factual recall: model X, top of SimpleQA, May 2026, self-reported"), and if you strip the bold name you are left with a true, dated, sourced sentence (Receipted Recommendation). Picks are authored, not computed, stored in the content layer's honestPicks[] so an editor controls every claim.
proposal.honestEmptyHandling:
Honest-empty is the EXPECTED, most common state (6 of 22 benchmarks honest-empty, 2 more self-reported), so it is first-class content with the same slot geometry as a populated pick, never a faded or apologetic fallback. Two layers:

1. On the EXPLORER: a use case whose backing boards are all honest-empty or self-reported is allowed to look thin and stays in the index at full weight, its backing-chips strip reading stone DATED chips. The thinness IS the warning (Honesty Density Made Visible). It is never hidden to make the grid look full.

2. On the USE-CASE PAGE picks block: where a sub-goal's board is honest-empty, the slot does NOT emit a model name. It renders an EmptyPick that reuses the PR1 HonestEmpty treatment (left-border dated-anchor block) inside the HonestPick frame:
- Line A claim slot reads "No trustworthy current board for this" with a stone DATED StatusChip.
- Body: one authored sentence explaining WHY no number survived (JS-only board, no independent eval, self-reported with a maintainer conflict), in GOV.UK-grade plain declarative voice.
- Anchor-only fallback: a single most-cited dated anchor in the stone-tier left-border block ("Human experts 53.7%, best direct model 50.1% at last reading, chance 25%") so the slot still teaches calibration and difficulty with zero invented number (Anchor-Only Fallback). Strictly a baseline, never dressed as a ranking.

Self-reported with conflict (OmniDocBench, whose #1 shares a maintainer with the benchmark) renders a populated HonestPick but downgraded: a CONTESTED StatusChip, a loud SELF-REPORTED FlagChip, and a pick caveat naming the maintainer conflict, so it can never display at the same confidence as an independent board (no self-reported laundering). Dated boards (BFCL, April 2026) render with a DATED chip and past-tense "led as of APR 2026" so a stale board never masquerades as today.

The four states (live / dated / self-reported-contested / truly-empty) stay visually and verbally distinct using the existing tier palette so they do not collapse into one dismissible grey (avoids caveat fatigue).
proposal.dataModel:
USE-CASES TAG on Benchmark (the cross-link axis):
- Add `useCases Json?  // string[] of use-case slugs this benchmark backs` to model Benchmark (prisma/schema.prisma ~line 551, beside relatedConcepts/sources). Json (not a relation table and not a new join model) to match the existing inline-Json convention (leaders, relatedConcepts, sources are all inline Json) and keep the schema delta to one nullable column. After db push/generate, kill and restart the dev server on 3100 (the .prisma/client HMR gotcha).
- Add `useCases?: string[]` to BenchmarkSeed (prisma/seed-data/benchmarks.ts) and tag every record. Add an allowed-set USE_CASE_SLUGS check to scripts/seed-benchmarks.ts beside the DOMAINS/SCORE_TYPES/TRUSTS validators, plus the BANNED_DASHES sweep already covers text. A benchmark may carry multiple slugs (HLE in science + factuality; LMArena in preference + writing); cross-tagging is plain shared membership.
- Read path in lib/benchmarks.ts: add useCases to the BenchmarkCardData select and type (parsed via the existing asStringArray helper).

USE-CASE CONTENT LAYER (the authored intro + picks). Chosen shape: a SECOND Prisma model `UseCase`, mirroring Benchmark's draft-gate + inline-Json shape (not a static map, so it gets the same admin publish path and dash-validation for free; not a Json blob on some other model, so it has its own slug route and @@index([status])):

model UseCase {
  id          String   @id @default(cuid())
  slug        String   @unique          // /benchmarks/use/[slug]
  title       String                     // "Factual accuracy: not making things up"
  audienceLine String                    // one-line student gloss (explorer + page lead)
  intro       String                     // the bold bus-stop lead / task framing prose
  evidenceLine String                    // "read mainly through SimpleQA, with HLE as a cross-check"
  benchmarkSlugs Json                     // string[] ordered backing benchmarks (the yardsticks)
  honestPicks Json                        // authored picks: [{ label, benchmarkSlug, leaderRef|null, verb, caveat, scaffoldNote?, empty?, datedAnchor? }]
  howToChoose Json                        // [{ goal, benchmarkSlug, guidance }]
  relatedConcepts Json?                   // reuse the {label,slug?} shape + live-catalog validation
  order       Int      @default(0)        // editorial sort on the explorer
  status      String   @default("draft")  // publish gate, mirrors Benchmark
  createdAt   DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt   DateTime @updatedAt
  @@index([status])
  @@map("use_cases")
}

proposal.motionTokensA11y:
Motion: reuse the PR1 vocabulary with no new keyframes where possible. UseCaseRow reuses the benchCellIn entrance + capped 18ms stagger + the .editorial-link / bench-name sweep, all already gated by usePrefersReducedMotion and the @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) rule copied into the explorer component's <style>. The use-case page reuses the .trend-detail-enter benchDetailFade 260ms and the .editorial-link svg translateX(3px) hover from benchmark-detail-client's <style>. HonestPick has no bespoke animation; it appears within the page fade. No new motion is introduced.

Tokens: strictly the verified PR1 set, nothing added. STATUS chips always use a -bg/-fg tile pair as a unit (sage LIVE #E2EED5/#4F7028, steel NEAR-CEILING #D8DEEC/#38486D, gold CONTESTED #FBE2A6/#855208, stone DATED #DDDDE3/#43434E). Ordinal/position badges only use tile-indigo #DDDAFB/#4F40A0 (never rank-quality). The gold caveat hue --color-gold #B8860C is the one structural recolor, reused for the pick caveat clause and the new SCAFFOLD FlagChip (tile=gold, sharing the Disputed hue so the inline-chip vocabulary stays SELF-REPORTED / DISPUTED / SCAFFOLD). Accent links resolve to the editorial #4255FF under data-surface=editorial. Page width --maxw-content 1040px. No --shadow-card, no new color token, no new card class.

Accessibility: full-row Links carry aria-label with the use-case/benchmark name (PR1 pattern); decorative badges/rules are aria-hidden; StatusChip carries the trust WORD as text so trust is never color-only (the page's core a11y promise); the as-of date sits in a title attribute on the date line (PR1 ScoreStamp pattern). Trust-tier-gated verbs mean the textual claim itself degrades with the evidence, so a screen-reader user hears "led as of APR 2026", not a bare crisp winner. Two-column is avoided on the use-case page, so there is no rail to drop on tablet; the explorer inherits the <=600px single-column collapse.
